title: Money model is incoherent and the ~50% platform take is hidden; the same favor shows $20 / $20.88 / $10
observation: A Tiny Favor reads $20 base + 2.9% + $0.30 = member pays $20.88 (checkout.tsx:97-100,240), but the pal's seeded payout is $10 (mockData.ts:103). There is no computePayout() — only buyer-side computeFees (types.ts:150-155) — and pal earnings are hand-entered with inconsistent take rates (36% on the $50 favor, 52% on the $20). The pal is then shown the member's gross invoice 'Total Cost $20.88' on their own active-favor screen (pal.tsx:159-186), which re-implements fee math inline instead of calling computeFees. Neither side ever sees the real spread.
impact: The single most important number for an earner overstates pay by ~2x in the misleading direction, the member is told fees are only 2.9%+$0.30 (implying the pal gets ~$20), and the platform's true revenue is hidden from both parties. For a real-money two-sided marketplace this is a trust-destroying transparency failure and a worker-pay-disclosure compliance risk.
suggestion: Define one canonical money model (base, member-facing fees, pal payout = base minus an explicit stated commission) in types/computeFees and render it everywhere. Show the member 'Pal receives $X / Service fee $Y' and show the pal a true 'You earn $Z (after platform commission)'. Never display the member's invoice total on a pal earnings/in-progress screen; drive all three screens from one helper so they agree.
concreteFix: Add a canonical payout helper to src/types/index.ts beside computeFees: export PLATFORM_COMMISSION_RATE and computePayout(base) returning { commission, payout }. Then drive all three surfaces from it: (1) tracking.tsx:104-107 — derive Payout from computePayout(activeFavor.price) instead of the hardcoded "$10.00"; (2) pal.tsx PalFavorInProgress (159-186) — delete the inline base*0.029/total math and the member "Total Cost $20.88" rows, and render a single "You earn $Z (after platform commission)" from computePayout; never show the buyer invoice on a pal screen; (3) mockData.ts earnings (101-104) — regenerate e1/e2 amounts from computePayout so the seed matches the model and the take rate is consistent across favors. Optionally add a "Pal receives $X / Service fee $Y" line to checkout.tsx SummaryBody so the member sees the real split rather than an implied $0.88 fee.
evidence: checkout.tsx:97-100,240; tracking.tsx:104-107; pal.tsx:159-161,183-186; src/types/index.ts:150-155 (no computePayout); src/data/mockData.ts:101-104 (earnings $33 / $10), :97-98 (member totals $51.75 / $20.88)

title: The booked pal and the member's actual favor are discarded; tracking screens render hardcoded literals
observation: requestFavor() builds activeFavor from the draft (store:114-134) but with no palId, and ProviderDetail's 'BOOK FAVOR PAL' only navigate('FavorTracking') with no store write (providers.tsx:189-194). FavorTracking never reads activeFavor — every value is a literal: 'Aditya Patil', 'Pick up package from Amazon Hub Lockers', '16 February 2023', 'Tiny Favor', '$10.00', '2099 Woodvine Rd, Lorman' (tracking.tsx:74-126). On the pal side the same data drifts across screens: Aditya->Stephanie->Aditya, date jumps two years, and the navigation destination is the PAL's own home address (pal.tsx:81-190; mockData.ts:27).
impact: A member who builds a $150 Huge favor and books a pal they picked sees a $10 Tiny package pickup for a stranger and a 2023 date; a pal is routed to their own house and shown two different members for one job. The confirmation of who is coming and what they'll do — the moment trust is decided — is completely broken and reads as fraudulent/broken.
suggestion: On booking, assign the chosen pal into activeFavor (assignPal(palId) -> palId + status='matched'), then drive every tracking/active screen from activeFavor + the resolved pal record (name/avatar/rating, tier label, price, description, location, etaWindow) instead of literals.
concreteFix: 1) store/index.tsx: add assignPal(palId) to StoreValue and impl: setActiveFavor(f => f ? {...f, palId, status:'matched'} : f); keep activeFavor as the single source. 2) providers.tsx:192: onPress={() => { assignPal(pal.id); navigation.navigate('FavorTracking'); }}. 3) tracking.tsx FavorTracking: pull const fav = activeFavor; const pal = pals.find(p=>p.id===fav?.palId); render Avatar={pal.avatar}, name=`${pal.firstName} ${pal.lastName}`, tier label=FAVOR_TIERS[fav.tier].label, payout=`$${fav.total ?? fav.price}`, description=fav.description (use ONE source for both rows), address=fav.location.address, date=format(fav.scheduledFor ?? fav.createdAt), window=fav.etaWindow — falling back to the current literals only when activeFavor is null. 4) pal.tsx Navigation + PalFavorInProgress: drive name/desc/date/address from activeFavor (set by acceptFavor) and resolve the member (favor.memberId); since 'u_steph' isn't in nearbyPals, add a member record/name onto the seed favor or a small resolver so Navigation no longer shows a hardcoded 'Stephanie'. Keep the exact Figma layout/styling; only swap the literal Text values for state. This preserves visual fidelity when no live favor exists (prototype/demo) while making a real booking flow through.
evidence: providers.tsx:192 (no store write on book); src/screens/tracking.tsx:68-126 (FavorTracking literals); src/screens/pal.tsx:122-124 (Navigation 'Stephanie') and 173-175,190 (PalFavorInProgress literals); src/store/index.tsx:114-134 (requestFavor sets no palId) and StoreValue 30-43 (no assignPal); src/data/mockData.ts:27,70 (member home vs real pickup)

title: The member's tracking screen wears Favor-Pal labels: 'CALL CLIENT', 'I'M HERE', 'Payout'
observation: FavorTracking is reached by the member tapping BOOK FAVOR PAL (providers.tsx:192) yet renders pal-side controls/copy: a money row 'Payout $10.00' (tracking.tsx:104-107) and CTAs 'CALL CLIENT' and 'I'M HERE' (tracking.tsx:130-131). A member has no 'client', isn't paid a 'payout', and is at their own location, so 'I'M HERE' is meaningless — and the member advances by declaring their own arrival straight to OrderComplete.
impact: On a core post-booking screen every action and the price are labeled for the wrong actor. 'Payout $10' next to a number the member just paid is alarming (implies a 50% take), 'CALL CLIENT' is nonsensical, and the member completes their own favor with no sense a pal did anything.
suggestion: Re-voice for the member: show the total they paid (not the pal's payout), relabel 'CALL CLIENT' -> 'Call your Pal', and replace 'I'M HERE' with a passive status-driven 'Pal arrived'/'Track' state. Keep the pal-voiced version only on the pal flow.
concreteFix: First flag to the user/designer that favor-booked-member.png is internally inconsistent (member header, pal-voiced actions), since changing it deviates from the Figma-exact mandate. If approved, in tracking.tsx: (1) line 105 relabel "Payout" -> "Total Paid" (or "You Paid"); (2) line 130 "CALL CLIENT" -> "CALL YOUR PAL" (ideally "Call Aditya"); (3) line 131 replace "I'M HERE" with a member-appropriate action — either "MESSAGE PAL" or a status-driven "Pal arrived"/"Track" label — and avoid having the member's tap be the thing that completes the favor (in the mock, drive completion from a pal-arrival status). Also note the whole screen is static (hardcoded $10.00/dog-walk/address) and doesn't reflect the member's actual request — worth wiring to the active favor in a follow-up.
evidence: src/screens/tracking.tsx:104-107 (Payout row), tracking.tsx:130-131 (CALL CLIENT / I'M HERE), tracking.tsx:25 (onComplete -> OrderComplete); entry at src/screens/providers.tsx:190-192; design figma-ref/favor-booked-member.png

title: Half the FavorStatus lifecycle is dead; the member self-completes a favor that never leaves 'requested', with no live status/ETA
observation: FavorStatus defines requested->matched->enroute->arrived->in_progress->completed (types.ts:42-51) but 'enroute'/'in_progress' are never assigned and advanceFavor is only ever called once, with 'arrived' (pal.tsx:133). Member-side, requestFavor sets 'requested' and nothing advances it: Searching has no timer/auto-match (checkout.tsx:259-295), the tracking window is a static '11:50AM-12:10PM' (tracking.tsx:88-90), and 'I'M HERE' jumps to OrderComplete. The stored etaWindow is never read.
impact: 'Is my pal actually coming, and when?' is unanswerable after payment — frozen window, no status timeline, no moving map. The status field is unreliable (En Route/In Progress badges can never legitimately appear) and a member can fabricate a 'completed', rated favor on demand with zero pal involvement.
suggestion: Drive screens off activeFavor.status and advance it explicitly (matched on booking, arrived/in_progress/completed via the pal's actions). Show a member status timeline (Accepted -> On the way -> Arrived -> In progress -> Done) using the existing etaWindow, and gate member rating behind a real pal-initiated 'completed' transition.
concreteFix: In tracking.tsx FavorTracking: read the member's own favor via useStore().activeFavor — replace hardcoded '11:50AM - 12:10PM' with activeFavor?.etaWindow, and drive description/address/tier/price/payout from activeFavor instead of the literal dog-walking placeholder. Add a member status timeline (Accepted -> On the way -> Arrived -> In progress -> Done) rendered from activeFavor.status. In checkout.tsx Searching, add a useEffect setTimeout that calls advanceFavor('matched') and routes into FavorTracking, then progressively advance enroute/arrived/in_progress (the pal side already owns arrived/in_progress/completed via pal.tsx). Critically, stop the member's tracking screen from self-completing: the member's primary action should be track/cancel (not the pal-language 'I'M HERE'); gate OrderComplete + rateFavor behind a real 'completed' transition (e.g., navigate to OrderComplete only when activeFavor.status === 'completed', which a pal action sets). This also lets En Route/In Progress states legitimately render.
evidence: src/store/index.tsx:114-160 (status only set to requested/matched/arrived/cancelled/completed; advanceFavor); src/screens/pal.tsx:133 (sole advanceFavor call); src/screens/tracking.tsx:25,88-90,131 (static window, I'M HERE->OrderComplete, ignores activeFavor); src/screens/checkout.tsx:259-295 (Searching, no timer/auto-match); src/screens/providers.tsx:192 (member BOOK FAVOR PAL -> FavorTracking without advancing status); types.ts:42-51,82 (FavorStatus, etaWindow); grep `status ===` = no matches (status never branched on)

title: Money is captured before a pal is confirmed, and the only forward path is disguised as an error-recovery button
observation: Tapping 'Pay US$20.88' calls requestFavor() and navigates straight to Searching (checkout.tsx:159-167) — payment happens before any pal is matched. Searching has no timer, no auto-advance, and exactly one CTA, 'CHOOSE ANOTHER FAVOR PAL' under 'Taking too long?' (checkout.tsx:283-291) — the happy path is mislabeled as a fallback, and the headline already names a specific pal while claiming to still be searching. There is no Cancel and no charge-timing reassurance.
impact: The funnel appears stalled at the most anxiety-inducing moment for a new payer; users won't realize they must tap the 'Choose another' button to proceed. Charging before a pal accepts, with no refund/authorize-then-capture language, reads as 'you've been charged, now hope someone shows up' — the most likely post-payment drop point.
suggestion: Authorize at pay time but capture only after a pal accepts, with copy 'You won't be charged until a Favor Pal accepts.' Add an explicit accept-confirmation state that auto-advances Searching to tracking, relabel the 'Choose another' button as secondary, and add a Cancel action on Searching.
concreteFix:
Fidelity-preserving fix (do this): in Searching (checkout.tsx:259) add a useEffect that simulates the pal accepting after a short delay — e.g. const t = setTimeout(() => { advanceFavor('matched'); navigation.replace('FavorTracking'); }, 3500); return () => clearTimeout(t);. Pull advanceFavor from useStore (already exported, store/index.tsx:136). Use navigation.replace so Back/gesture doesn't dump the user back onto a stale Searching modal. This makes the designed happy path work while keeping "CHOOSE ANOTHER FAVOR PAL" as the genuine impatient-user fallback, and leaves the screen pixel-identical to searching.png.

Optional, design-gated (do NOT ship without design sign-off since it deviates from Figma): add reassurance copy "You won't be charged until a Favor Pal accepts," switch the mock toward authorize-then-capture semantics, and add a Cancel affordance on Searching. Keep these separate from the auto-advance fix.
evidence: src/screens/checkout.tsx:159-167 (pay -> requestFavor -> navigate Searching) and src/screens/checkout.tsx:259-295 (Searching, no auto-advance); flow chain at src/navigation/index.tsx:78-82 and src/screens/providers.tsx:63,192; mock no-charge at src/store/index.tsx:114-134; design at figma-ref/searching.png

title: One-tap cancellation of an already-paid favor with no confirmation, refund logic, or fee disclosure
observation: After money is committed, 'CANCEL FAVOR' (tracking.tsx:92) and the pal's 'CANCEL THIS FAVOR' (pal.tsx:136) both call cancelFavor() and navigate away immediately. cancelFavor (store:140-145) merely moves the favor to history as 'cancelled' — no refund, no fee, no receipt, no confirmation dialog (contrast Settings' delete-account confirm). The words 'refund'/'cancellation fee' appear nowhere in src.
impact: A single accidental tap silently cancels a paid favor with zero feedback about whether the member is refunded or charged, or whether the pal is compensated. Undisclosed cancellation charges/refunds are a top driver of chargebacks, disputes, and regulator complaints — a hard launch blocker.
suggestion: Add a confirmation step stating the cancellation policy and exact refund/fee outcome ('You'll be refunded $X' or 'A $Y fee applies because a pal is en route'), then perform the corresponding refund/charge and write an explicit ledger entry. Make free-cancel-window vs fee thresholds part of the money model.
concreteFix: Two layers. (1) Quick win (S): wrap both cancel handlers in a confirmation that states the outcome. Reuse the existing Alert.alert pattern from profile.tsx (or InfoModal) — e.g. onCancel in tracking.tsx:26 becomes Alert.alert('Cancel favor?', message, [{text:'Keep favor',style:'cancel'},{text:'Cancel favor',style:'destructive',onPress:doCancel}]) where message reflects the refund/fee outcome. Same for pal.tsx:136. (2) Money model (M): add cancellation semantics to types/index.ts alongside computeFees — a helper computeCancellation(favor) that returns {refund, fee} keyed off favor.status: free full refund while status is requested/searching/matched (no pal en route), a disclosed fee once status is enroute/arrived. Change store cancelFavor (index.tsx:140) to cancelFavor(reason?) that computes refund/fee from activeFavor.status, writes a Transaction ledger entry into the existing transactions array (Transaction type already exists), and stores the refund/fee on the cancelled favor record. After cancel, surface the result via InfoModal ('You were refunded $X' / 'A $Y cancellation fee applied because a pal was en route'). Mirror compensation messaging on the pal side.
evidence: tracking.tsx:26-29,92 (member onCancel); pal.tsx:136-138 (pal cancel); store/index.tsx:140-145 (cancelFavor — no refund/fee/ledger); types/index.ts (computeFees — no cancellation policy); contrast: profile.tsx:295-303 (Alert.alert delete-account confirm) and components/index.tsx:268 (reusable InfoModal)

title: The same completed favor shows a different fabricated pal in the list vs the detail
observation: Because palId is never persisted (see track-01), the History list shows generic 'Favor Pal' with initials (history.tsx:93-94,104, no fallback), while the detail for the SAME favor falls back to s.pals[0] and confidently renders 'Fabrizio L.', his photo, 4.9 rating, '92% Reliable', '100% Positive Reviews' (history.tsx:157) — an identity unrelated to the favor.
impact: Inconsistent, fabricated provider identity within one record erodes trust and is a data-integrity bug: the receipt attributes a real person, rating and reliability stats to a favor they never performed.
suggestion: Persist palId at booking time and share one resolution policy; in detail render an explicit 'Favor Pal'/unknown state instead of silently defaulting to pals[0]. Never invent a provider via array-index fallback.
concreteFix: Two parts. Part 1 persist the selected pal at booking: add a store action bookPal(palId) that sets activeFavor.palId, and call it in providers.tsx ProviderDetail BOOK FAVOR PAL onPress (lines 189-194) before navigating to FavorTracking. rateFavor already preserves palId via the spread, so it then flows into history and list and detail agree. Part 2 stop fabricating identity in the detail: in history.tsx:157 remove the s.pals[0] fallback; when pal is undefined render the explicit Favor Pal header (palName already handles this at line 161) and suppress the rating, percent Reliable, and percent Positive Reviews rows (lines 224-248) instead of borrowing pals[0] stats, mirroring the honest list behavior.
evidence: src/screens/history.tsx:157 (detail s.pals[0] fallback), :93-94, :224-248; src/screens/providers.tsx:189-194 (BOOK FAVOR PAL discards selection); src/store/index.tsx:114-134 (requestFavor sets no palId), :147-152 (rateFavor)

title: Pal can't decline (no-op re-offers the same job), accept is one irreversible info-less tap, and there's no way back into an active favor
observation: ACCEPT permanently filters the favor out of incomingFavors and sets activeFavor (store:154-160), but 'decline this favor' only calls goBack() (pal.tsx:95-97) — it never removes the favor, so Home re-offers the identical job. 'View More' is plain Text with no handler (pal.tsx:86). The pal accepts blind (no distance/ETA, member rating, duration, or net payout). Home only ever surfaces incomingFavors[0] (dashboard.tsx:133,180-202), so a pal mid-favor has no resume card; cancelling pushes a 'cancelled' history row but never re-queues the favor.
impact: Asymmetric, irreversible commitment: the safe action (decline) is the visually weakest and functionally broken while the committing action is one undo-less tap with no information. A pal who navigates Home mid-favor is stranded, and cancelled favors silently vanish from the marketplace.
suggestion: Add a real declineFavor(id) that removes it (and surfaces the next offer), give decline equal visual weight, and put decision-critical facts (distance/ETA, member rating, est. duration, net payout) on the quick-view. Render an 'active favor' resume card on Home when activeFavor exists, and re-queue favors on pal cancel.
concreteFix: store/index.tsx: add declineFavor(id) => setIncomingFavors(list => list.filter(x => x.id !== id)) (add to StoreValue interface, value object, and useMemo deps), and make cancelFavor re-queue: setIncomingFavors(q => [{ ...f, status: 'requested', palId: undefined }, ...q]) alongside (or instead of) the cancelled history row. pal.tsx:95-97: decline -> s.declineFavor(favor.id); navigation.goBack(); and give the link equal visual weight (e.g., a bordered button matching ACCEPT). pal.tsx:86: wrap 'View More' in TouchableOpacity that opens the full read-only detail (reuse the PalFavorInProgress layout, or surface favor.etaWindow + net payout from computeFees + member rating in an expanded section). dashboard.tsx (isPal branch ~180-202): when s.activeFavor exists, render a 'Resume active favor' card that navigates to the screen matching activeFavor.status (matched -> 'Navigation', arrived -> 'PalFavorInProgress'), taking priority over the incoming-favor card.
evidence: src/screens/pal.tsx:86,89-97; src/store/index.tsx:140-145,154-160; src/screens/dashboard.tsx:132,180-202; src/navigation/index.tsx:84-88

title: 'You just got paid!' shows no amount, and completing a favor never adds to Earning History
observation: PalFavorSuccess is a green check + 'You just got paid!' with no dollar figure, tip, or favor reference (pal.tsx:223-237). rateFavor pushes the favor to history (store:147-152) but earnings is a frozen const with no setter — const [earnings] = useState(seed.earnings) (store:73) — so the just-completed job never appears in Earning History.
impact: The core earner reward moment lands flat and is unverifiable: the celebration omits the amount and the ledger never records the job, so a pal cannot reconcile what they actually made.
suggestion: Put the net payout (and any tip) on the Success screen ('You earned $10.00 (+$2 tip)'), make earnings stateful, and append a Transaction on completion so the job immediately appears in Earning History with a 'queued for payout' confirmation.
concreteFix: Fix ONLY the ledger half; do NOT touch the Success screen (changing it breaks Figma fidelity). In src/store/index.tsx:73 make earnings stateful: const [earnings, setEarnings] = useState(seed.earnings). In rateFavor (store:147-152), when the completed favor was performed by the current pal (f.palId set / user.role === 'pal'), append a Transaction: { id: nextId('t'), favorId: f.id, title: <tier label>, amount: f.price + (tip ?? 0), status: 'completed', date: now(), kind: 'earning' } via setEarnings((e) => [tx, ...e]). It then surfaces immediately in Earning History (payouts.tsx groupByMonth). If a payout amount is desired in-flow, add it to the member-facing receipt/OrderComplete, not to pal-success which must stay design-exact.
evidence: src/screens/pal.tsx:223-237 (Success — Figma-faithful, leave unchanged); src/store/index.tsx:73 (immutable earnings, no setter); src/store/index.tsx:147-152 (rateFavor updates only history); src/screens/payouts.tsx:101-103 (Earnings reads static earnings)

title: No balance or payout timing, and every earning is mislabeled 'Apple Pay'
observation: Earning History is a flat month-grouped list (payouts.tsx:101-148) with no running total, available balance, pending amount, or payout cadence/next-payout date anywhere. Every row is stamped 'Apple Pay' with an Apple logo badge (payouts.tsx:67-74,133-135) — but pals receive money via their connected bank/Stripe account; Apple Pay is how the MEMBER pays in.
impact: 'When do I get paid?' is unanswerable for an earner, and labeling payouts 'Apple Pay' conflates the member's payment instrument with the pal's bank deposit, undermining confidence that money is actually flowing to their account.
suggestion: Add an earnings header (total earned, current balance, 'Next payout: <date> to Bank ****789') and a one-line cadence note ('Payouts arrive 2-3 business days after a favor is completed'). Replace the per-row 'Apple Pay' label with the actual payout destination.
concreteFix: Two-part, sequence by risk. (1) Low-risk, high-trust, ship now: replace the per-row "Apple Pay" subtitle (payouts.tsx:133-135) and the ApplePayBadge (67-74, used at 128) with the actual payout destination so it matches the app's own model — e.g. subtitle "Bank ****789" or "Stripe *1234" sourced from the same connected-account value shown in StripeOnboarding (line 168), and swap the apple logo badge for a bank/card glyph. (2) Product enhancement that deviates from the Figma, so confirm with design before adding: insert a summary header above the groups in the ScrollView (payouts.tsx:118-122) with total earned, available balance, pending, and a "Next payout: <date> to Bank ****789" line plus a one-line cadence note ("Payouts arrive 2-3 business days after completion"). Compute totals from useStore().earnings (sum of item.amount). Because the screen is currently Figma-exact, treat (2) as an explicit design change rather than a silent fidelity fix.
evidence: src/screens/payouts.tsx:67-74 (ApplePayBadge), :128 + :133-135 (per-row "Apple Pay" label), :101-148 (Earnings list, no balance/timing), :118-122 (ScrollView header insertion point); internal-model contradiction: :168 (Stripe *1234) and :201-208 (BankInfo bank fields). Design ground truth: figma-ref/earnings.png.

title: Payout setup looks 'done' before it starts: pre-connected Stripe, pre-filled fake bank account, saves with no validation
observation: The Account screen shows 'Stripe *1234' as connected from the first visit (payouts.tsx:166-169), so a brand-new pal appears payout-ready without onboarding. BankInfo pre-fills Account Number and Confirm both as '123 - 456 - 789' (payouts.tsx:206-207), performs no validation (routing length, account==confirm, required fields), and SAVE just navigate('Tabs') with no persistence, success confirmation, identity/tax step, or security copy (payouts.tsx:255).
impact: The flow both over-claims (already connected before setup) and under-delivers (a pal taps SAVE on a pre-filled fake account and believes they're set up to get paid), giving zero confidence at the moment an earner hands over bank details.
suggestion: Start unconnected ('Set up payouts to get paid'), don't pre-fill account numbers, validate routing/account/confirm match, and on SAVE show 'Bank account connected' and flip Stripe to the connected state. Add brief security/identity-verification copy.
concreteFix: In BankInfo's SAVE handler (payouts.tsx:255), add a validate() before navigating: normalize accountNumber/confirmAccount (strip spaces/dashes) and require they match, require routing to be 9 digits, and require non-empty Account/Bank name; on failure render an inline error row (or reuse InfoModal). On success, show a brief confirmation (an InfoModal "Bank account connected" or a toast) before navigate('Tabs'). To address the trust angle without breaking Figma fidelity, add a payoutAccount field to the store (store/index.tsx) set on first successful save, and drive StripeOnboarding's "Stripe *1234" row (payouts.tsx:166-169) from that state with a "Set up payouts to get paid" fallback when unset — preserving the exact connected visual once configured. Do NOT remove the Figma pre-fill values, since those match the reference image and the project requires Figma-exact screens; keep them as editable defaults but gate SAVE behind validation.
evidence: C:\Users\Ibrahim Syed\my-favor\src\screens\payouts.tsx:201-260 (BankInfo: SAVE at :255, pre-fill at :206-207) and :166-169 (hardcoded Stripe row in StripeOnboarding)

title: Availability status is written but never read — Home always says 'YOU'RE ONLINE', entry points always show a red dot + 'Set Status', and status doesn't gate work
observation: setStatus writes user.status (online/invisible/offline) and SetStatus pre-selects it (profile.tsx:491-518; store:108), but nothing else reads it. The pal Home bar unconditionally renders 'YOU'RE ONLINE' (dashboard.tsx:200) and offers the incoming favor regardless of status (default is 'offline', mockData.ts:29). Profile and the drawer hardcode a red dot and the literal 'Set Status' (profile.tsx:124-126,437-443,538). There is no availability scheduling anywhere.
impact: 'Am I online and earning right now?' is the foundational pal state and it is invisible/incoherent — an offline pal is told they're online and still gets offered jobs, and the status picker has no observable effect. Members get no confirmation a status change stuck.
suggestion: Drive the Home bar, dot color, and label from user.status (Online/Invisible/Offline), only surface incoming favors when online, and reflect status in both the Profile header chip and drawer identity block. Consider a simple availability schedule (days/time windows).
concreteFix: In dashboard.tsx, derive `const status = s.user?.status ?? 'offline'` and replace the unconditional bar at lines 198-202 with a status-driven label+color map (online → "YOU'RE ONLINE"/green, invisible → "YOU'RE INVISIBLE"/gray, offline → "YOU'RE OFFLINE"/muted); wrap that bar in a TouchableOpacity that navigates to 'SetStatus' so pals can toggle from Home. Gate the incoming-favor card (line 180) on `incoming && status === 'online'` so offline/invisible pals aren't offered work. In profile.tsx, tint `st.statusDot` (line 538) by status at the Profile header (124-126) and SideDrawer (437-443) while keeping the Figma-faithful "Set Status" label. Optionally set mockData.ts:29 default to 'online' so the initial pal state is coherent. Scheduling (day/time windows) is a follow-up, not required for this fix.
evidence: src/screens/dashboard.tsx:180,198-202; src/screens/profile.tsx:124-126,437-443,494; src/store/index.tsx:108; src/data/mockData.ts:29

title: Role-switch label is logically inverted between Home and Profile — same state, opposite copy
observation: The two screens use exact-inverse ternaries. Home: pillText = isPal ? 'Switch to request a favor' : 'Switch to be a Favor Pal' (dashboard.tsx:131). Profile: switchLabel = isPal ? 'Switch to be a Favor Pal' : 'Switch to request a favor' (profile.tsx:109). So a pal sees 'Switch to request a favor' on the map but 'Switch to be a Favor Pal' on Profile — told to become what they already are — while the Profile toggle shows value={isPal} ON next to that contradictory text.
impact: The primary mode-switch control gives contradictory instructions depending on where it's tapped, eroding confidence in what the toggle does and risking an accidental wrong-direction switch.
suggestion: Make the label describe the destination action consistently; adopt Home's logic everywhere and extract one roleSwitchLabel(role) helper so the two screens can't drift again.
concreteFix: In profile.tsx:109 adopt Home's (correct) logic: `const switchLabel = isPal ? 'Switch to request a favor' : 'Switch to be a Favor Pal';`. To prevent future drift, extract a single helper such as `roleSwitchLabel(role: Role)` (e.g. in src/types/index.ts or src/store/index.tsx) returning the destination-action label, and call it from both dashboard.tsx (pillText) and profile.tsx (switchLabel) so the two screens can't diverge again.
evidence: src/screens/profile.tsx:109 (inverted); src/screens/dashboard.tsx:131 (correct reference); toggle action profile.tsx:110 / dashboard.tsx:134; Switch value profile.tsx:138

title: Role switch uses two inconsistent toggles, gives no feedback, and the tab bar never adapts to the pal role
observation: setRole just flips user.role (store:107) with no navigation change, confirmation, or status transition. It's exposed via two different controls — a custom knob pill on Home (dashboard.tsx:163-170) and a native iOS Switch on Profile (profile.tsx:135-143). Both roles see the same four tabs (navigation/index.tsx:45-48); Earnings and the payout Account are only inside the Home-only drawer and only when role==='pal'.
impact: The two-sided value prop is the product's core, yet switching feels inert: same tabs, no 'you are now a pal' confirmation, and earnings buried two layers deep. Two different toggle styles for one action erode polish and make the model harder to learn.
suggestion: Make role a first-class mode: confirm on switch and swap the tab set (member: Home/Messages/History/Profile; pal: Home/Messages/Earnings/Profile) so Earnings is a tab in pal mode. Use one consistent switch component in both places and reflect the active role in a persistent header label.
concreteFix: 1) Fix the inverted label bug: change profile.tsx:109 to match the Home logic (isPal ? 'Switch to request a favor' : 'Switch to be a Favor Pal'). 2) Extract one RoleSwitch component into src/components/index.tsx (label + control + onToggle) and consume it in both dashboard.tsx:163-170 and profile.tsx:135-143 so the control style and copy are identical. 3) Make the tab set role-aware: in navigation/index.tsx Tabs(), read role from useStore and render an Earnings tab in place of History when role==='pal' (member: Home/Messages/History/Profile; pal: Home/Messages/Earnings/Profile), adding Earnings to TabParamList + TAB_ICON. This lifts Earnings out of the drawer for pals. 4) Optional polish: in store setRole, surface a lightweight confirmation/toast (or reflect the active role in a header label) so the mode change is acknowledged.
evidence: store/index.tsx:107; dashboard.tsx:131,163-170; profile.tsx:108-110,135-143,452-460; navigation/index.tsx:26-49

title: Half the app's destinations are gated behind a drawer that only opens from the Home tab; Profile reaches no account controls
observation: The app runs a 4-item tab bar plus a hamburger SideDrawer, but the hamburger renders only on the Home/map screens (dashboard.tsx:154, pal.tsx:45) — Messages/History/Profile have none. Settings, Help, payment 'Account', and Logout exist ONLY in the drawer (profile.tsx:449-464); the Profile tab's rows all route to EditProfile (profile.tsx:153-156). So from Profile a user cannot reach Settings, Help, cards, or Logout at all. The drawer also duplicates two tabs (View Profile, Favor History), and Profile's back chevron calls navigate('SideDrawer') so 'back' opens a left drawer (profile.tsx:116).
impact: Settings/Logout/Help/cards are conventionally under Profile/Account; here they're a hidden Home-only path, so users tap Profile expecting account controls and hit a dead end. The tab/drawer split is neither additive nor hierarchical and feels like two overlapping nav systems.
suggestion: Pick one secondary-nav model: surface Settings + Payment + Help + Logout as rows on the Profile tab (making it the account hub), or keep the drawer but expose the hamburger on every tab. Remove the back chevron from root tab screens and don't repurpose 'back' to open the drawer.
concreteFix: Keep the Figma-faithful drawer but resolve the dual-nav conflict in two contained edits. (1) Make Profile the account hub: in profile.tsx Profile, replace the four EditProfile-only InfoRows (153-156) with genuine destinations — keep Email/Phone/Home/Password on EditProfile but add a secondary section of rows for Settings, Payment (members) / StripeOnboarding+Earnings (pals), Help, and Logout (reuse confirmDelete/s.logout patterns already in Settings, profile.tsx:295-334). (2) Fix the root-tab back chevron: in DarkHeader usage at profile.tsx:114-119, drop onBack on the Profile tab (root tabs should not show a back chevron) — render the pencil-only header, or if a left affordance is desired, make it an explicit hamburger that navigates('SideDrawer') rather than overloading 'back'. Optionally, for consistency, expose the same hamburger->SideDrawer affordance on Messages (messages.tsx:43) and History (history.tsx:90) headers so the drawer isn't Home-only, and either wire tracking.tsx:35's menu button to navigate('SideDrawer') or remove it. If Profile becomes the hub, also remove the now-redundant View Profile / Favor History rows from the drawer (profile.tsx:434,449) to stop the tab duplication.
evidence: src/screens/profile.tsx:116 (back->SideDrawer), :153-156 (rows all->EditProfile), :449-464 (drawer is sole home of Settings/Help/Account/Logout); src/navigation/index.tsx:45-48 (4-tab bar); src/screens/dashboard.tsx:156 and src/screens/pal.tsx:45 (only drawer triggers); src/screens/tracking.tsx:35 (dead menu button)

title: One menu label points to two different screens depending on a role toggle set elsewhere
observation: The drawer's 'Account' row routes to Payment for members but StripeOnboarding for pals (profile.tsx:456-460), and the 'Earnings' row appears/disappears based on the same role flag (profile.tsx:452). So the menu's contents and a stable-looking label's destination change silently with a toggle that lives on another screen.
impact: Menu instability breaks spatial memory: a user who learned 'Account = my saved cards' lands on Stripe bank onboarding after switching roles, and disappearing rows make the app feel unpredictable.
suggestion: Use distinct, stable, role-appropriate labels ('Payment Methods' vs 'Payouts & Bank'), and avoid hiding Earnings — disable or contextualize it rather than swapping one row's target.
concreteFix: In the SideDrawer menu (profile.tsx:452-460), stop overloading one 'Account' row. Since the same account is both member and pal, render two stable, distinctly-labeled rows for everyone: 'Payment Methods' -> Payment (card icon) and 'Payouts & Bank' -> StripeOnboarding (cash icon), so neither financial surface becomes unreachable after a role toggle. Keep 'Earnings' visible always but contextualize it (e.g. route pals to Earnings and members to a short 'Become a Favor Pal to earn' explainer, or disable with a hint) instead of removing the row, so the menu's row set stays constant and spatial memory holds. If strict figma fidelity to the single 'Account' row is required, at minimum rename per role ('Payment Methods' for members, 'Payouts' for pals) so a stable-looking label never silently changes destination.
evidence: src/screens/profile.tsx:452-460 (SideDrawer menu rows); role toggle at dashboard.tsx:134,163 and profile.tsx:110,139; design ref figma-ref/side-drawer.png

title: Two stacked bottom bars on Home, with a dead, redundant 'home' button
observation: Home is wrapped in a Tab.Navigator that draws the OS tab bar (navigation/index.tsx:45) AND paints its own floating action bar at bottom:16 (dashboard.tsx:174) with a red home icon + 'REQUEST A FAVOR'. The red home square is a plain View with no onPress in both member and pal layouts (dashboard.tsx:177,206). In Figma there is no native tab bar — that floating bar IS the intended nav — so the added Tab bar makes the home button doubly redundant.
impact: Wasted vertical space, a confusing double navigation surface, and a prominent button-looking element that does nothing when tapped.
suggestion: Either drop the Tab bar on the Home route and keep the Figma floating bar as primary nav, or keep the Tab bar and remove the redundant red home button. At minimum make the home square interactive or delete it.
concreteFix: Pick one of two clean fixes. (A) If keeping the OS tab bar as primary nav: delete the redundant red home square from dashboard.tsx (the <View style={styles.homeBtn}> blocks at lines 177-179 and 206-208) so the floating bar becomes a single-purpose 'REQUEST A FAVOR' / status CTA, and reclaim the space. (B) If keeping the Figma floating bar as primary nav: hide the tab bar on the Home route via screenOptions tabBarStyle:{display:'none'} for the Home tab in navigation/index.tsx (Messages/History/Profile remain reachable via the existing hamburger->SideDrawer). Minimum viable fix: give the home square an onPress (e.g. navigation.navigate('SideDrawer') or a scroll/reset) so it is no longer an inert button-looking element. Option A is the lowest-risk and best matches the current tab-based IA.
evidence: src/screens/dashboard.tsx:174 (floating bar), :177-179 & :206-208 (inert homeBtn View); src/navigation/index.tsx:36-49,45 (Tab.Navigator + Home tab)

title: Multiple prominent nav/contact controls are decorative and do nothing
observation: FavorTracking renders a hamburger with no onPress (tracking.tsx:35), a static 'Switch to request a favor' pill (tracking.tsx:38-43), and a bottom faux tab bar whose SHARE and ACTIVITY items have no handlers (tracking.tsx:136-155). On the pal flow MapTopBar's switch pill is a non-interactive View (pal.tsx:48), the 'banner' variant removes the hamburger entirely during active navigation (pal.tsx:38-42), and 'Call About This Favor' has no onPress (pal.tsx:130) — the primary way to contact the member before arrival is dead.
impact: Tappable-looking affordances that silently fail teach users the UI is unreliable; a member mid-tracking who taps the menu/switch gets nothing, and the pal loses drawer access and a working call button exactly when an active favor is on screen.
suggestion: Wire these or remove them: make the tracking hamburger open the drawer, make SHARE/ACTIVITY and 'Call About This Favor' functional, make the role pills real toggles, and keep the hamburger present in the pal banner state.
concreteFix: Wire the genuinely-dead handlers (all small): (1) tracking.tsx:35 — add onPress={() => navigation.navigate('SideDrawer')} to the menuBtn, mirroring dashboard.tsx:156. (2) tracking.tsx:130 CALL CLIENT and pal.tsx:130 'Call About This Favor' — pass a real onPress that calls Linking.openURL(`tel:${phone}`) or, given the mock backend, opens an InfoModal ('Connecting call…') so the affordance gives feedback; for pal, pass onPress into the ActionRow exactly like the working Message row at 131. (3) tracking.tsx:137/151 — wire SHARE to React Native's Share.share(...) and ACTIVITY to the History tab (navigation.navigate('Tabs', { screen: 'History' })), or, if they are meant to be pure chrome, render them as non-touchable Views so they stop looking tappable. (4) Switch pills (tracking.tsx:38-43, pal.tsx:48): acceptable to leave as Figma-faithful decoration; if making interactive, route to the role switcher. Do NOT 'fix' the banner-state hamburger removal (pal.tsx:38-42) — it matches favor-booked.png.
evidence: src/screens/tracking.tsx:35 (dead hamburger), :130 (CALL CLIENT onPress={() => {}}), :137 & :151 (SHARE/ACTIVITY no onPress); src/screens/pal.tsx:130 (Call About This Favor missing onPress) vs :131 (working Message row), :48 (switch pill View); reference: src/screens/dashboard.tsx:156 (working hamburger pattern), src/navigation/index.tsx:94 (SideDrawer route)

title: The favor's location is non-editable and silently defaults to a hardcoded Mississippi address never collected at signup
observation: ConfirmAddress shows address = user?.homeAddress ?? '2099 Woodvine Rd, Lorman, MS' with no field to change it — only X and 'Confirm Address' (request.tsx:320,348-385). Signup never asks for an address (authflow.tsx:179-286), and a new account inherits seed.currentUser.homeAddress = '2099 Woodvine Rd, Lorman, MS' (mockData.ts:27).
impact: A new member anywhere would dispatch a pal to a random Mississippi address with no way to correct it — a safety/fulfillment failure and a refund/dispute generator. 'Confirm Address' implies review when the user literally can't edit it.
suggestion: Make the address editable on ConfirmAddress (tap to search/enter, or use device location) and/or collect a home/service address during onboarding so the prefill is actually the user's.
concreteFix: In authflow.tsx Signup add a Home/Service Address field and include it in s.signup(...) so verifyOtp carries a real address instead of the seed default. In request.tsx ConfirmAddress, make the address row tappable to edit (a TextInput bound to draft state, or navigate to EditProfile, or device geolocation) so the user can review/change before dispatch — currently 'Confirm Address' implies review the user can't perform inline. Finally, fix the seed data so the demo isn't self-contradictory: align mockData.ts:27 homeAddress and request.tsx:324 coords with the seed user's Miami, FL profile (mockData.ts:24-26).
evidence: request.tsx:320,348-385,324; authflow.tsx:179-286; store/index.tsx:87; mockData.ts:24-27; profile.tsx:198,220,256 (EditProfile already edits homeAddress)

title: Scheduling is stubbed to NOW-only: the 'Where to? NOW' pill is inert and mislabeled, and scheduledFor is never set despite the schema
observation: The clock-icon row reads 'Where to?' with a value pill 'NOW' (request.tsx:375-378) — the words ask about a place on a screen already titled 'Location of your favor', while the clock+NOW express timing. Tapping it does nothing. The schema supports scheduledFor and the seed even sets it (types.ts:70, mockData.ts:71), but requestFavor reads an always-undefined draftFavor.scheduledFor (store:127). The app's own example use case is 'walk my dog around 4pm'.
impact: Two location-sounding labels stacked on one sheet (the second meaning time) makes scheduling undiscoverable, and on-demand-only excludes the most common errand occasions and removes the natural relief valve for no-supply moments.
suggestion: Relabel to 'When?' with value 'Now'/'Schedule', make the chip open a now/schedule picker that persists scheduledFor, show scheduled favors to pals as a claimable queue, and offer scheduling as the fallback when no pal is available now. If unsupported, remove the row.
concreteFix: Preferred (matches user's Figma-exact priority): delete the entire row at request.tsx:362-380 (the View containing the time-outline icon, "Where to?" text, and "NOW" pill), leaving the card identical to figma-ref/confirm-address.png (location line + Confirm Address). This removes the dead control and the mislabel in one stroke. If product instead wants scheduling kept: relabel request.tsx:375 from "Where to?" to "When?", convert the pill View (377-379) to a TouchableOpacity that opens a Now/Schedule picker and calls s.setDraft({ scheduledFor }) so store/index.tsx:127 populates the already-plumbed field; toggle the pill text between "Now" and the chosen time. Either way the current inert/mislabeled state should not ship.
evidence: C:\Users\Ibrahim Syed\my-favor\src\screens\request.tsx:362-380 (inert mislabeled row; pill 377-379 has no onPress); C:\Users\Ibrahim Syed\my-favor\src\store\index.tsx:127 (reads always-undefined draftFavor.scheduledFor); C:\Users\Ibrahim Syed\my-favor\src\types\index.ts:70 (schema field); C:\Users\Ibrahim Syed\my-favor\figma-ref\confirm-address.png (design has no such row)

title: A blank favor description silently becomes lorem-ipsum that is shown as the real task
observation: FavorDescription's NEXT is never gated on a non-empty value (request.tsx:205-207), and useFavorSummary substitutes FALLBACK_DESC = 'Sum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit...' when the description is empty (checkout.tsx:25-27,42), rendering Latin under the Description heading the member pays for and saving it to the favor. The same lorem string is the pal quick-view fallback (pal.tsx:83). clearDraft exists but is never called (store:112), so abandoned drafts persist.
impact: A member who skips the box pays for a request shown as fake Latin, and the pal would receive meaningless instructions — a direct quality/trust failure at the final review step, compounded by stale drafts bleeding across sessions.
suggestion: Gate NEXT on a trimmed non-empty (min-length) description, replace both fallbacks with honest copy ('No description added.' / 'No details provided yet.'), and call clearDraft() after a favor is requested or abandoned.
concreteFix: In request.tsx, gate navigation in FavorDescription.onNext (and Negotiate.onNext at 230-233, which also carries description): block or disable NEXT when desc.trim() is empty or below a small min length, with inline validation copy. Once NEXT is gated the member-flow summary always has real text, so the checkout.tsx:42 FALLBACK_DESC becomes unreachable in normal use; replace it (and FALLBACK_ADDRESS) with honest empty-state copy like No description added for defense-in-depth instead of Latin, keeping the lorem only as the greyed Field placeholder to preserve the Figma look. Fix pal.tsx:83 to use a truthiness check with honest copy so empty strings are caught, not just null/undefined. Finally, call clearDraft() after a successful requestFavor() in checkout.tsx pay() and on flow abandon so stale drafts do not pre-fill the next request.
evidence: request.tsx:165-168,205-207 (and Negotiate 230-233); checkout.tsx:42,110-112; src/store/index.tsx:122 (persists empty string), 112 (clearDraft unused); pal.tsx:83; figma-ref/favor-summary.png (source of the lorem text)

title: Terms acceptance is decorative — signup isn't gated on it — and Privacy/Terms links are dead
observation: Signup tracks an 'I agree to Terms & Conditions' checkbox (authflow.tsx:187) but SIGNUP fires regardless of agree (authflow.tsx:282) — the state is only used to render the checkmark. The Settings 'Privacy Policy' and 'Terms of Service' rows have empty handlers (profile.tsx:323-324).
impact: A financial-services contract requires a recorded affirmative acceptance of Terms (and an accessible privacy policy) before transacting. A non-functional consent checkbox and dead legal links mean there is no enforceable agreement governing payments, liability, refunds, or arbitration — a clear compliance blocker and legal exposure.
suggestion: Gate the SIGNUP button on the agree checkbox and persist the accepted ToS version/timestamp with the account; make the Privacy Policy and Terms rows open the actual documents.
concreteFix: 1) authflow.tsx:282 — add `disabled={!agree}` to the SIGNUP Button so it cannot fire without consent (the Button already honors `disabled`, see profile.tsx:401). 2) Make the "Terms & Conditions" text at authflow.tsx:278 tappable (wrap in TouchableOpacity / pressable Text) opening the ToS. 3) Record acceptance: extend store `signup()` (src/store/index.tsx) and its payload type (src/types/index.ts) with `termsAcceptedAt: Date.now()` and a `termsVersion` constant, set inside onSignup (authflow.tsx:189-192). 4) profile.tsx:323-324 — replace the empty handlers with real navigation: either `Linking.openURL(PRIVACY_URL)` / `Linking.openURL(TERMS_URL)`, or a lightweight in-app legal screen (WebView/markdown) reused by both the signup link and these rows. For the prototype, even a stub LegalDoc route with placeholder copy is enough to remove the dead-end taps.
evidence: src/screens/authflow.tsx:187,189-192,278,282; src/screens/profile.tsx:323-324; Button `disabled` precedent at src/screens/profile.tsx:401

title: OTP screen is a dead-end: wrong/partial codes navigate nowhere with no error, no back/close, and a fake timer/resend
observation: verifyOtp only sets the user when code.length===4 (store:84-92), but onVerify ignores the returned boolean and always navigate('Tabs') (authflow.tsx:312-315); with fewer than 4 digits the user is never set so the tap silently does nothing and no error shows. The modal has no back/close affordance, 'Resend' merely clears the boxes (authflow.tsx:346), the '00:43' timer is a static string, and nothing says which email/phone the code was sent to.
impact: Users who mistype or never receive a code get zero feedback and no escape hatch at the most critical onboarding moment, and a frozen '00:43' looks broken — a hard trap that produces abandoned signups and support tickets.
suggestion: Show an inline 'Incorrect code' error when verifyOtp returns false and only navigate on success; add a back/close control; make Resend re-trigger the (mock) send and reset a real countdown; and add destination context ('We sent a code to {masked}').
concreteFix: In authflow.tsx OtpVerify: (1) Disable VERIFY until code.every(Boolean) and, in onVerify, branch on the returned boolean — `const ok = await s.verifyOtp(code.join('')); if (!ok) { setError('Please enter the full 4-digit code'); return; }` Drop the explicit navigate('Tabs') entirely (or keep as fallback) since setting the user already swaps the conditional stack to Tabs automatically. Render the error in the empty space below the boxes so no layout shift / design deviation. (2) Make the timer real: useState seconds initialized to 43, a useEffect setInterval countdown formatted mm:ss into the existing "Code expires in {t}" line. (3) Resend: reset the boxes AND reset the countdown to 43 and re-call the mock send (add a resendOtp() to the store, or reuse signup(pendingSignup)); disable Resend while seconds>0. (4) Optional destination context only if willing to deviate from Figma — expose pendingSignup (or user.email/phone) from the store and mask it. Code pointers: authflow.tsx:312-315, 325-326, 343-348; store/index.tsx:84-92; navigation/index.tsx:57-70.
evidence: src/screens/authflow.tsx:312-315 (onVerify), 325-326 (static timer), 346 (Resend); src/store/index.tsx:84-92 (verifyOtp); src/navigation/index.tsx:57-70 (conditional stack — Tabs absent when unauthenticated, making navigate a no-op)

title: A member can pay for a favor with no payment method selected
observation: SelectPayment.pay guards only on pals.length and never checks that a card is selected; selected defaults to cards[0]?.id ?? null (checkout.tsx:156-167). After deleting the last card the only tile is '+ Add', selected is null, yet 'Pay US$20.88' still runs requestFavor() and routes to Searching with no card on file.
impact: A core marketplace invariant — a paid transaction must have a funding source — is violated, eroding trust in a money-handling flow.
suggestion: Disable the Pay button until a card is selected and cards.length>0.
concreteFix: In checkout.tsx SelectPayment, derive a valid selection instead of trusting raw state: selectedCard = cards.find(c => c.id === selected); canPay = Boolean(selectedCard). Early-return in pay() when not canPay (keep the existing pals guard). On the Pay TouchableOpacity (checkout.tsx:238) add disabled={!canPay} and dim it via opacity canPay ? 1 : 0.5. This also covers the stale-selection case where the previously selected card was deleted from the Payment screen.
evidence: src/screens/checkout.tsx:156 (selected init), 159-167 (pay guard), 238 (always-tappable Pay button); src/screens/payment.tsx:49 (removeCard trash); src/store/index.tsx:114-134 (requestFavor ignores card), 170 (removeCard no floor); src/data/mockData.ts:92-94 (single seeded card)

title: Destructive deletes are inconsistent: card delete has no confirmation; Delete Account uses Alert.alert which no-ops on web
observation: The card trash icon calls removeCard immediately with no confirm and no default reassignment (payment.tsx:49-51; store:170), and the edit/delete icons sit directly adjacent so a mis-tap deletes a saved card (accessibility: 22px icons, hitSlop 8). Meanwhile Settings.confirmDelete relies on Alert.alert (profile.tsx:295-303), which is unimplemented on react-native-web — so on the stated web target tapping 'Delete Account' shows nothing. The cross-platform InfoModal already exists (components:268).
impact: On web a primary account action is a dead button while card deletion is one stray tap from irreversible loss — inconsistent, trust-breaking handling of destructive actions.
suggestion: Replace Alert.alert with a cross-platform confirm built on InfoModal, and use the same confirm gate before removeCard; on removal of a default card, promote another to default. Space the edit/delete icons apart.
concreteFix: Add a cross-platform confirm primitive: extend InfoModal (components/index.tsx:268) with optional `confirmLabel`/`cancelLabel`/`onConfirm`, or add a `ConfirmModal` sibling with two buttons over the same scrim. (1) Settings (profile.tsx:295-303): drop Alert.alert; drive a `const [confirmDelete,setConfirmDelete]=useState(false)` state, render ConfirmModal, onConfirm -> s.logout(). (2) Payment (payment.tsx:49): set a `pendingDeleteId` on trash tap, render the same ConfirmModal, onConfirm -> removeCard(id). (3) store removeCard (index.tsx:170): after filtering, if the removed card was the default and others remain, set the first remaining card's isDefault=true. (4) Increase separation between pencil/trash (larger marginRight or padding on each TouchableOpacity) to reduce mis-tap.
evidence: src/screens/payment.tsx:46-51; src/store/index.tsx:163-170; src/screens/profile.tsx:295-303; src/components/index.tsx:268-289; node_modules/react-native-web/dist/exports/Alert/index.js (static alert(){})

title: Payment screen has a dead 'Payment History' row and an 'edit' pencil that opens a blank AddCard instead of editing the card
observation: The 'Payment History' row renders a chevron but is a plain View with no onPress (payment.tsx:69-73) — it looks tappable and does nothing. The per-card edit pencil routes to AddCard (payment.tsx:46), which mounts a blank form prefilled with hardcoded dummy data rather than the selected card's values, so 'edit' actually starts a new Visa.
impact: For a user managing their wallet, a primary row is broken and 'edit' silently creates duplicates/wrong data — exactly where users are least tolerant of ambiguity.
suggestion: Make Payment History navigate (or remove its chevron), and pass the card id into AddCard and prefill from it for a true edit.
concreteFix: In payment.tsx: (a) wrap the Payment History row (69-73) in a TouchableOpacity that navigates to a real destination (e.g. navigation.navigate('History') or a FavorHistoryDetail) — or, if no list screen is intended yet, remove the chevron so it doesn't signal tappability. (b) For edit: change types.ts:48 to AddCard: { cardId?: string }; pass navigation.navigate('AddCard', { cardId: card.id }) at line 46; in AddCard read route.params?.cardId, look the card up from the store, and prefill the fields you actually persist (brand/last4/exp) — leaving full PAN/CVC blank since they aren't stored. Add an updateCard(id, patch) action to the store and call it in handleSubmit when cardId is present (preserving the existing brand instead of hardcoding 'visa') instead of addCard. If a true edit is out of scope, the minimal honest fix is to drop the pencil entirely so 'edit' can't silently create a duplicate.
evidence: src/screens/payment.tsx:46 (edit pencil -> AddCard, no params), payment.tsx:69-73 (dead Payment History View), payment.tsx:88-97 & 109 (hardcoded dummy prefill + always-add brand 'visa'); src/navigation/types.ts:48 (AddCard: undefined)

title: AddCard ships broken default values — Zip is 'Chicago', the default card is expired, brand is always Visa
observation: AddCard initial state sets zip = 'Chicago' (payment.tsx:96) — a city in the Zip field — and a default expiry '01/2025' (payment.tsx:90), already past given today is 2026. handleSubmit hardcodes brand: 'visa' regardless of the number entered (payment.tsx:109), with no card-number/expiry formatting or validation.
impact: A user adding a card sees a Zip reading 'Chicago' and an expired date, and any Mastercard/Amex is recorded and displayed as Visa — it reads as unfinished and would fail real Stripe validation.
suggestion: Default Zip to a real postal value or empty, set a future/blank expiry with MM/YY placeholder, derive brand from the card-number prefix, and add light formatting/validation so the saved list shows correct brand + last4.
concreteFix: In payment.tsx handleSubmit (line 101-112), replace the hardcoded brand: 'visa' with a derivation from the card-number prefix on the already-computed `digits`. Add a tiny helper, e.g. const brand = digits.startsWith('4') ? 'visa' : /^(34|37)/.test(digits) ? 'amex' : /^(5[1-5]|2[2-7])/.test(digits) ? 'mastercard' : digits.startsWith('6') ? 'discover' : 'card'; then await addCard({ brand, last4, expMonth, expYear }). Optionally add light card-number group formatting on change. Leave the Zip='Chicago' (line 96) and expiration='01/2025' (line 90) prefills as-is to preserve Figma fidelity; if the team wants the saved card to not read as expired, the minimal non-fidelity-breaking option is to bump the default expYear at submit time, but that is a product call, not a bug to silently change.
evidence: C:\Users\Ibrahim Syed\my-favor\src\screens\payment.tsx:109 (brand hardcode); :88-97 (prefills matching figma-ref/add-card.png); :37,43 (brand rendered in Payment list)

title: Async submit buttons have no loading/disable guard — double-taps fire duplicate actions and navigations
observation: onLogin, onSignup, onVerify and pay are all async with a 600ms mock delay yet none disable the button or track loading, even though the shared Button supports loading/disabled (components:96-118) and AddCard already uses a saving flag (payment.tsx:108-111). A quick double-tap calls the store method and navigation.navigate twice (e.g. two Searching screens, requestFavor fired twice). None are wrapped in try/catch, so a real-network rejection would leave the screen stuck.
impact: On real networks impatient users will double-tap, producing duplicate favor requests/charges and a corrupted navigation stack — the classic 'I tapped once, why did it submit twice' bug.
suggestion: Add a submitting state to each async handler, pass it as Button loading/disabled, and wrap the awaited call in try/catch that surfaces an error and re-enables the button. Reuse the AddCard pattern.
concreteFix: Reuse the AddCard pattern. In Login/Signup/OtpVerify add a `const [submitting, setSubmitting] = useState(false)`; in each handler do `if (submitting) return; setSubmitting(true); try { await s.login(...); navigation.navigate(...) } catch (e) { /* surface error */ } finally { setSubmitting(false) }` and pass `loading={submitting}` to the shared Button (it already disables + shows ActivityIndicator). For SelectPayment.pay the visible control is a custom TouchableOpacity (checkout.tsx:238-243), not the shared Button, so guard it manually: add `disabled` + opacity to that TouchableOpacity and an in-flight ref/state to make pay() reentrancy-safe (early-return if already paying). Keeping the try/catch is cheap insurance for when login/requestFavor hit a real backend.
evidence: authflow.tsx:67-70 (onLogin), 189-192 (onSignup), 312-315 (onVerify); checkout.tsx:159-167 (pay) + 238-243 (custom pay TouchableOpacity); existing pattern: payment.tsx:98,108-111,160 (AddCard saving); Button supports it at components/index.tsx:98,106; delays at store/index.tsx:11,80,85,95 (note requestFavor at 114-134 has none)

title: Change Password is a dead-end — the form collects passwords but onSave never submits them
observation: EditProfile renders Current/New Password fields and keeps currentPw/newPw state (profile.tsx:202-203,268-274), but onSave builds updateProfile({firstName...zip, avatar}) with no password (profile.tsx:217-223) — they're dropped with no validation or feedback. Every Profile/Settings 'Change Password' affordance funnels here (profile.tsx:156,311). onSave also accepts cleared email/phone, persisting blank contact fields.
impact: A security-relevant action gives a false success signal — the user types a new password, taps SAVE, the screen dismisses, and they believe it changed when nothing happened. Blank-email saves break a key contact field.
suggestion: Wire currentPw/newPw into a real changePassword (validate current + min-length new + confirm, surface 'Password updated'), or hide the fields until supported. Validate email/phone and block empty values.
concreteFix: In profile.tsx onSave (217-223): before saving, if newPw is non-empty require currentPw non-empty and newPw.length >= a minimum, and add an inline error or Alert on failure; on success surface 'Password updated' feedback. Add a changePassword(currentPw, newPw) to store/index.tsx (even as a mock that validates and resolves) and call it from onSave instead of silently dropping the fields. Add email (regex) and non-empty phone validation and block the save when they are empty so updateProfile never persists blank contact fields. Add a Confirm Password field if a real change flow is intended. Lower-effort alternative consistent with the mock backend: remove the two password fields from EditProfile and point the 'Change Password' affordances (profile.tsx:156,311) at a dedicated stub or hide them until supported, so no false-success signal is given.
evidence: src/screens/profile.tsx:217-223 (onSave drops password), 268-274 (password fields), 156 & 311 (entry points); src/store/index.tsx:102-105 (updateProfile merge, no changePassword), :94 (login ignores password)

title: logout() leaks favor/draft state into the next session and never restores consumed inventory
observation: logout is just setUser(null) (store:100) — it doesn't clear draftFavor/activeFavor or restore incomingFavors, and acceptFavor permanently filters the accepted favor out of incomingFavors (store:154-160). So if user A drafts/requests/accepts and logs out, user B inherits A's in-progress activeFavor and half-built draft, and the seed favor A consumed stays gone.
impact: Cross-account data bleed (one user sees another's active favor and draft) and non-deterministic seed state after the first session — real correctness/privacy problems once more than one login happens.
suggestion: On logout, reset session-scoped state: clear draftFavor/activeFavor and re-seed incomingFavors/history, or key all per-user state by user id and wipe on logout.
concreteFix: In src/store/index.tsx, make logout reset all session-scoped state instead of only the user: const logout = useCallback(() => { setUser(null); setPendingSignup(null); setDraftFavor(null); setActiveFavor(null); setHistory(seed.history); setIncomingFavors(seed.favorsSeed); setCards(seed.cards); setTransactions(seed.transactions); setThreads(seed.threads); setMessages(seed.messages); setNotifications(seed.notifications); }, []); (all setters already exist except setEarnings which is fine to leave). Equivalent alternative: key the StoreProvider on auth so it remounts and re-seeds. Separately, call clearDraft() (or reset draftFavor) inside/after requestFavor (line 132) so a completed request does not pre-fill the next one. Severity is low for the mock, but this becomes the right behavior the moment a real multi-account backend replaces the seed identity.
evidence: src/store/index.tsx:100 (logout), :154-160 (acceptFavor filter), :67-76 (persisted session state); App.tsx:49 + src/navigation/index.tsx:57 (provider never remounts on logout); request.tsx:153 / dashboard.tsx:132 (stale state surfaces)

title: Tipping is broken: 'Other' is inert, tips never move money, and a post-payment tip charges with no confirmation
observation: The 'Other' tip chip maps to value: undefined with no custom-amount input (tracking.tsx:167-168,216-218), so selecting it visually highlights yet tips nothing. The chosen tip is stored on the favor (rateFavor, store:147-152) and only shown as '+ tip $X' in history (history.tsx:269) — it's never added to a transaction, the member's charged total, or the pal's earnings. The member already paid at checkout, so a tip adds a charge with no 'you'll be charged an additional $X' confirmation.
impact: A member who wants to tip more than $6 picks 'Other', sees it selected, and tips nothing; even fixed tips never reach the pal or any ledger. Charging a card after the fact without confirmation invites disputes.
suggestion: Render a numeric input when 'Other' is selected (validate > $0), show the new total and an explicit confirm before charging, and fold 100% of tips into the charged total and the pal's payout/earning ledger.
concreteFix: In tracking.tsx OrderComplete: when tipKey==='other', render a numeric Field (keyboardType numeric) and parse/validate to a number > 0, feeding setTip; disable submit until a valid custom amount is entered. Before charging, show the new total (favor.total + tip) with an explicit 'You'll be charged an additional $X' confirm. In store/index.tsx rateFavor (147-152): fold the tip into the stored favor.total and append a Transaction plus an earnings entry for the pal — which requires giving `earnings` (line 73) a real setter so the pal's payout ledger can be updated. Also fix history.tsx:265 to show a total that includes the tip so the breakdown reconciles.
evidence: src/screens/tracking.tsx:164-183,210-225; src/store/index.tsx:73,147-152; src/screens/history.tsx:265-270; src/screens/checkout.tsx:159-167,238-240

title: No pal vetting or supply-side onboarding: no ID/background check, no skills/categories, payout not gated before going online
observation: Choosing 'BE A FAVOR PAL' sets role and routes into the exact same name/email/phone/password signup as a member (onboarding.tsx:64-67; authflow.tsx:179-286), then into the same Tabs. Pal profiles show rating/reliability/% positive (providers.tsx:140-153) but there is no 'ID verified'/'background checked' badge anywhere, no skills or service categories, and StripeOnboarding/BankInfo are not required before going online.
impact: This is a marketplace where strangers enter members' homes; identity verification and background screening are table-stakes. Their absence — plus no verified-status signal and no categories to match on — is both a real safety risk and a credibility/match-quality gap that blocks real-money launch and insurer/partner due diligence.
suggestion: Add a distinct pal application: government-ID/selfie + background check, service categories/skills/intro photos, and gate 'go online' behind completed payout setup. Surface 'ID verified / background checked' badges and categories on ProviderResults/ProviderDetail and in history.
concreteFix: 1) Extend the data model in src/types/index.ts: add to User a verification block (idVerified: boolean, backgroundCheckStatus: 'none'|'pending'|'cleared', payoutReady: boolean) and pal supply attributes (categories: string[], skills/intro photos). 2) Branch onboarding by role: in authflow.tsx Signup, when s.user?.role === 'pal' route after OTP into a new PalApplication flow (government-ID + selfie capture stub, background-check consent, category/skill multi-select, intro photo) rather than straight to Tabs; keep the member path unchanged. 3) Gate availability in src/store/index.tsx: have setStatus('online') and acceptFavor() refuse (or surface InfoModal) unless idVerified && backgroundCheckStatus==='cleared' && payoutReady, and make BankInfo/StripeOnboarding a required step in the pal flow instead of an optional wallet-icon detour in payouts.tsx. 4) Surface trust signals: add an 'ID verified / background checked' badge and category chips next to the rating block in providers.tsx ProviderResults (the palCard, ~line 65-77) and ProviderDetail (the header stat rows, lines 140-153), and echo the badge in history.tsx FavorHistoryDetail. Mock the verification/background-check calls in the store the same way Stripe/Twilio are stubbed.
evidence: src/screens/onboarding.tsx:64-67 (role pick -> shared signup); src/screens/authflow.tsx:179-286 (single Signup form, no pal branch); src/types/index.ts:7-27 (User has no verification/skills/payout fields); src/screens/providers.tsx:140-153,160-165 (stats + hardcoded bio, no badge/categories); src/store/index.tsx:108 setStatus & 154-160 acceptFavor (ungated); src/screens/payouts.tsx (StripeOnboarding/BankInfo optional, never required)

title: Raw card and bank details are captured by custom forms into app state instead of Stripe SDK tokenization/Connect
observation: AddCard is a hand-rolled form holding the full PAN, CVC and billing address in component state (payment.tsx:88-97), submitting via addCard which keeps brand/last4/exp (store:163-168) — the raw card passes through the app. BankInfo collects routing/account/'confirm account' in plain state (payouts.tsx:201-223) even though a connected 'Stripe *1234' is shown (payouts.tsx:168), bypassing Stripe Connect. The only security signal in the flow is a lock icon on the Pay button (checkout.tsx:242).
impact: Collecting raw PAN/CVC and bank credentials through your own UI puts the app/server in PCI-DSS scope — exactly what Stripe Elements/PaymentSheet and Connect exist to avoid. As built it cannot pass a real launch security review, and the absence of 'secured by Stripe' signaling undermines trust at the highest-anxiety moment.
suggestion: Replace AddCard with Stripe PaymentSheet/CardField (tokenize on-device; never hold PAN/CVC in app state) and BankInfo with Stripe Connect onboarding so routing/account go straight to Stripe. Add 'Secured by Stripe' signaling on both forms.
concreteFix: payment.tsx (AddCard): wrap the screen in <StripeProvider publishableKey> and replace the Name/Card Number/Expiration/CVC Fields with @stripe/stripe-react-native CardField (or presentPaymentSheet). On submit call createPaymentMethod/confirmSetupIntent so tokenization happens on-device; pass only the returned paymentMethod id plus Stripe's card.brand/last4/exp to addCard — never store the PAN/CVC in component state. Keep the store's last4-only persistence (store:163-168) but source it from Stripe's response. Add a "Secured by Stripe" lockup under the form. payouts.tsx (BankInfo): replace the routing/account/confirm DarkFields with Stripe Connect onboarding — open a Connect account link via expo-web-browser (or the Connect SDK) from the StripeOnboarding "Edit Bank Information" row so routing/account numbers go straight to Stripe and never touch app state; the existing "Stripe *1234" row already implies a Connect external account to hang this off. For the prototype interim, at minimum stop prefilling a real-format PAN/routing and label the forms as stubs.
evidence: src/screens/payment.tsx:88-112 (AddCard state + handleSubmit); src/screens/payouts.tsx:201-223 (BankInfo state) and payouts.tsx:168,172-180 (connected Stripe row + Edit route); src/store/index.tsx:163-168 (addCard persists last4 only)

title: No in-favor safety toolkit (SOS / share-trip / emergency contact); the 'SHARE' control is inert
observation: While a stranger is en route to or inside the member's home, and while the pal is at a stranger's location, there is no SOS, no 'share live status with a friend', and no emergency-contact affordance. The bottom-bar 'SHARE' item that looks like share-trip has no onPress (tracking.tsx:137-140). The pal is also never shown the member's rating/verification before or during the meeting. grep finds no 'SOS'/'emergency' anywhere.
impact: Apps that send strangers to each other's homes ship a prominent safety toolkit because the live-meeting moment is where harm occurs. Tracking UI with no panic/share/emergency path — and a share control that's visually present but dead — is a critical safety gap and a likely insurer/app-store requirement.
suggestion: Add a safety toolkit on every live-favor screen: one-tap emergency, 'share my live favor status & location', and a saved emergency contact; show each party's rating/verification before meeting; and wire the existing SHARE control to share-trip.
concreteFix: Quick wins (S): wire the inert SHARE in tracking.tsx:137-140 to React Native Share.share() to share a live-favor status/ETA string, and give the inert ACTIVITY item (151-154) an onPress to History. Product (M): add a reusable SafetyBar component rendered on every live-favor screen (FavorTracking, pal Navigation pal.tsx:106-142, PalFavorInProgress pal.tsx:157-197) offering one-tap emergency (Linking.openURL('tel:911') behind a confirm InfoModal), 'Share my live favor status and location' (Share.share), and a saved emergency contact persisted in src/store with an editable field in Settings/EditProfile. Trust (S): render the counterparty rating/verification badge on the pre-meeting and in-progress profile rows — reuse StarRating already imported in tracking.tsx and the star pattern in pal.tsx.
evidence: src/screens/tracking.tsx:137-140 (inert SHARE), tracking.tsx:151-154 (inert ACTIVITY); src/screens/pal.tsx:106-142 (Navigation — no safety/rating), pal.tsx:157-197 (PalFavorInProgress — no safety/rating)

title: No way to report, block, or dispute a user or a charge
observation: There is no report/block/dispute mechanism (grep: no 'report'/'block'/'dispute'). The MessageThread overflow '...' button — the natural home for Report/Block — has no handler (messages.tsx:167-169). OrderComplete collects only rating/tip/free-text (tracking.tsx:171-253), and FavorHistoryDetail offers only a generic 'Need help?' box (history.tsx:306-320) with nothing structured for disputes/refunds.
impact: A real-money marketplace must let users report bad actors, block contact, and dispute charges in-app — for safety and to intercept disputes before they become chargebacks. Routing everything through a freeform 'contact support' box means no triage, no audit trail, and no enforcement loop.
suggestion: Add Report/Block to the MessageThread overflow and to each profile, and a structured 'Report an issue / dispute this charge' entry on OrderComplete and FavorHistoryDetail that creates a ticket (favor ID, category, evidence) feeding a moderation/dispute queue.
concreteFix: 1) Wire the MessageThread overflow '...' (messages.tsx:167) to open an action sheet (reuse InfoModal from src/components) exposing 'Report user' and 'Block user'; add reportUser(userId, reason)/blockUser(userId) actions to src/store/index.tsx that push to a mock moderation queue and (for block) hide threads. 2) Add the same Report/Block affordance to ProviderDetail (providers.tsx) and PalFavorDetail (pal.tsx) profile headers. 3) Replace the freeform/no-op support box on FavorHistoryDetail (history.tsx:305-320) and add an entry on OrderComplete (tracking.tsx) with a structured 'Report an issue / dispute this charge' flow: category picker + auto-attached favor ID + optional evidence, submitting to a createDispute(favorId, category, detail) store action that creates a ticket (rather than calling setMessage('')).
evidence: src/screens/messages.tsx:167-169; src/screens/tracking.tsx:171-253; src/screens/history.tsx:305-320 (SEND no-op at :320); no report/block/dispute actions in src/store/index.tsx

title: Personal phone numbers and the exact home address are exposed to strangers with no masking or approximate-location option
observation: Call actions ('CALL CLIENT' tracking.tsx:130, 'Call About This Favor' pal.tsx:130) dial directly, and the model stores/displays full phone numbers (mockData.ts:24; profile.tsx:154), implying real numbers are shared rather than proxied. The member's exact street address is attached to the favor and shown verbatim to the pal ('2099 Woodvine Rd, Lorman', pal.tsx:190, tracking.tsx:126), with no approximate-location-until-matched and no consent framing.
impact: Number masking and progressive address disclosure are standard because exposing a personal phone number and exact home address to an unvetted stranger creates stalking/harassment risk and privacy-law exposure — and for tasks like a locker pickup the precise home address is unnecessary data sharing.
suggestion: Route calls/SMS through a masked proxy number, reveal the precise address only after a pal is matched (show an approximate area beforehand), add a consent note when sharing a home address, and offer meet-at-door vs curbside.
concreteFix: Scope the fix to the address half (drop the unsupported phone-proxy claim). In the Favor/draft model (src/types/index.ts + store), carry both an approximateArea label (e.g. "Lorman, MS area") and the exact address. Pre-match surfaces — ConfirmAddress (request.tsx:317-360), ProviderResults, and the incoming PalFavorDetail (pal.tsx:65) — should show only approximateArea; reveal the exact street address (pal.tsx:190 Section, Navigation) only after acceptFavor() so disclosure is gated on a match. Add a one-line consent caption near the address field in ConfirmAddress ("Your exact address is shared with your Pal once matched"). For comms: when call/message are wired up later, do NOT add a raw tel: link to user.phone — route through a masked-proxy field on the favor and display a masked number; for now leave the no-op buttons but label intent. Low priority given the stubbed/prototype state.
evidence: src/screens/pal.tsx:130 (no-op call), pal.tsx:190 (exact address to Pal); src/screens/tracking.tsx:130 (no-op CALL CLIENT), tracking.tsx:124-126; src/screens/request.tsx:320-324 (full address on draft); src/screens/profile.tsx:154 (user's own phone); src/data/mockData.ts:21,39-54 (phones never shown to counterparty)

title: Zero-supply is a terminal dead-end at the END of the funnel, with no liquidity recovery
observation: The only handling of 'no pals nearby' is a terminal modal that fires AFTER tier->description->address->summary->Pay (checkout.tsx:159-167,246-251, no-favor-pal.png): 'there are no FavorPals in your area... try again later'. The dashboard gives no advance signal of supply — RadiusPin renders only the user's own avatar pin, never the nearby pals already in the store (dashboard.tsx:89-118).
impact: Discovering zero-supply only at the bottom of a 5-screen funnel maximizes wasted intent and drop-off, and the dead-end converts none of that demand — no notify-me, schedule-for-later, widen-radius, or broadcast.
