suggestion: Turn the dead-end into a recovery flow (post as 'notify me when a pal is available' or schedule-for-later, offer widen-radius/broadcast) and surface a live 'X pals nearby' count on the dashboard and before checkout so expectations are set at the top of the funnel.
concreteFix: Scope to two pieces. (A) In-scope, low-risk: in dashboard.tsx, plot `s.pals` as avatar pins inside the RadiusPin circle (data-driven, replacing/augmenting the decorative DarkMap Poi dots) and add a small 'N Favor Pals nearby' count chip near the request bar so supply is set at the top of the funnel using data already in the store. (B) Recovery: since the terminal modal is mandated by no-favor-pal.png, keep its visual but make the CTA actionable instead of dismiss-in-place — e.g. a 'Notify me when a Pal is available' button that saves the draft and routes back to Home, rather than `onClose` only (checkout.tsx:248-251). Separately, to make the state reachable/testable at all, the hard-coded `pals: seed.nearbyPals` (store/index.tsx:190) needs to become dynamic. Treat full schedule-for-later/widen-radius/broadcast as a separate product epic, not part of this fidelity rebuild.
evidence: src/screens/checkout.tsx:157,161-167,246-251 (terminal no-pal modal); src/screens/dashboard.tsx:89-118 (RadiusPin shows only user avatar, no pal pins); src/store/index.tsx:190 + src/data/mockData.ts:37-58 (pals always non-empty -> zero-supply branch unreachable)

title: One-sided pricing plus a ~50% take rate will trigger a decline spiral and supply churn
observation: The member unilaterally sets price from 4 fixed tiers ($20/$50/$100/$150) with only 'amount of effort' as guidance (types.ts:32-40; request.tsx:113-127); the pal can only ACCEPT or decline, never counter (pal.tsx:89-97), and there is no real two-way negotiation despite the 'Negotiate' screen. The displayed pal payout is ~50% of member price ($10 of $20) on top of the member's 2.9%+$0.30 fee.
impact: Members will under-tier large jobs (no effort->tier mapping) while pals can only reject, producing a match-failure spiral; a ~50% effective take is far above category norms (~15-30%) and, with no pal-side fee transparency before accepting, will crater supply retention.
suggestion: Let pals counter-offer or set their own rate (or add transparent distance/demand pricing), recommend a tier from the favor description, and disclose + rebalance the take rate with a pal-side fee breakdown shown before ACCEPT.
concreteFix: Make pal economics explicit and consistent, then add a counter affordance. (1) In types.ts add a single source of truth, e.g. `export const PAL_PAYOUT_RATE = 0.8; export function computePayout(base, tip=0){ return Math.round((base*PAL_PAYOUT_RATE + tip)*100)/100; }`, and rebalance toward category norms (~15-25% take). (2) In pal.tsx PalFavorDetail (line 68) replace the member-price headline with the pal's net, e.g. "You earn $X" plus a small "member pays $Y" sub-line, and add a payout breakdown row before ACCEPT. (3) In pal.tsx PalFavorInProgress (lines 159-161,183-186) swap the member-side "Service Fee 2.9% / Transaction Fee / Total Cost $20.88" block for a pal-side breakdown (gross, platform fee, net payout). (4) Drive tracking.tsx:107 and mockData.ts:101-104 from computePayout() so the $10.00/$33.00 literals stop disagreeing (currently 50% vs 66%). (5) Optionally add a "Counter offer" button next to ACCEPT on PalFavorDetail and an effort->tier hint on SelectFavor (request.tsx:113-127).
evidence: pal.tsx:65-101 (esp. line 68 headline; lines 89-97 accept/decline); pal.tsx:157-197 (member-side fee block at 159-161,183-186); tracking.tsx:99-108 (hardcoded $10.00 payout); mockData.ts:101-104 (inconsistent earnings 50% vs 66%); types.ts:32-40,150-155; request.tsx:85-142,218-306

title: Members pick a price tier with no examples, then pay, all before seeing who will do the favor; no step indicator
observation: SelectFavor offers Tiny $20 / Small $50 / Big $100 / Huge $150 with only 'Choose the cost of favor based on the amount of effort required' — no examples, so a member can't tell whether a dog walk is Tiny or Small (request.tsx:113-127). Payment is captured at SelectPayment before any provider is shown (pals first appear after paying), and there's no step indicator across the 5 request screens.
impact: Two confidence problems for a new user: guessing the right price with no guidance (over/under-paying), and committing money to a faceless system before seeing a single person's face/rating/reviews — the opposite of how trust is normally built.
suggestion: Add concrete examples to each tier ('Tiny — quick errands like a dog walk or coffee run'), let the member preview/choose a pal before capturing payment or clearly state 'You'll choose your Favor Pal next', and add a step indicator (Step 2 of 5).
concreteFix: Ship the small, non-design-conflicting piece: add a one-line textual example caption under each tier title in TierCard (request.tsx:65-70), e.g. tiny="Quick errands — dog walk, coffee run", small="~1hr tasks — groceries, yard tidy", big="Half-day help — furniture build, deep clean", huge="Big jobs — small move, event setup". Source the strings from FAVOR_TIERS (add an `example` field in types/index.ts:36-39) so member and summary stay in sync. Separately, add a reassuring subtitle near FavorSummary's PAY NOW / SelectPayment header (checkout.tsx:137-142,194) such as "You'll be matched with a Favor Pal next — you can review and switch before they start." Treat the cross-screen step indicator and the pay/pal reorder as optional product-direction changes, not fixes, since both contradict the current Figma flow.
evidence: src/screens/request.tsx:113-134 (SelectFavor tier list, generic guidance line, illustration-only examples); src/types/index.ts:32-40 (fixed tier prices); src/screens/checkout.tsx:142,159-167,279,286-290 (pay → Searching → ProviderResults ordering, auto-named pal); src/navigation/index.tsx:72-81 (request flow order); figma-ref/select-favor.png

title: Retention/matching engine missing: a notification model exists but there is no inbox and no push
observation: The store defines notifications + markNotificationRead and ships seed notifications (store:58-59,76,182-185; mockData.ts:118-121), but nothing renders them — no notification center, no Notifications route, and no expo-notifications integration. Pals only discover nearby requests by actively watching the dashboard incoming card (dashboard.tsx:180-197).
impact: Push is the primary re-engagement and matching mechanism for on-demand marketplaces ('new $20 favor near you', 'your pal arrived'). Without it, matching only works while the app is foregrounded — throttling liquidity and forfeiting the cheapest retention lever for both sides.
suggestion: Wire expo-notifications, add a notification inbox bound to the existing store data, and trigger push on match/arrival/new-nearby-request/cancellation, with pal job-alert subscriptions by category and radius.
concreteFix: Pick one of two in-scope actions; do NOT build the proposed expo-notifications + job-alert-subscription engine (out of scope for a mock, Figma-exact prototype and undemoable in Expo Go). Option A (remove dead code): delete the orphaned notifications/markNotificationRead from StoreValue and StoreProvider (store/index.tsx:58-59,76,182-185,193,198) and seed.notifications (data/mockData.ts:118-121) since no design screen consumes them, and either remove the "Push Notifications" Settings row or wire its value into store state so it is not a silent no-op (profile.tsx:289,314). Option B (surface existing data, if product confirms the feature): add a small Notifications inbox screen bound to the existing store notifications + markNotificationRead, register it in navigation/index.tsx, and reach it via a bell icon with an unread badge in the dashboard header (dashboard.tsx top bar near the role pill at lines 162-170). Either way, treat real push as a documented stub alongside the other mocked services.
evidence: src/store/index.tsx:58-59,76,182-185; src/data/mockData.ts:118-121; src/screens/profile.tsx:289,314; src/screens/dashboard.tsx:180-202; src/navigation/index.tsx (no Notifications route)

title: Reputation is hollow (identical reviews/bios per pal) and there is no rebook/favorite loop
observation: ProviderDetail renders the SAME three seedReviews and an identical hardcoded bio for every pal (providers.tsx:160-187); reputation is a single star number with no per-pal review storage, completion rate, or badges. FavorHistoryDetail ends with a generic support box and no 'request again' (history.tsx:305-321), and there is no favorites/saved-pal concept in types or store.
impact: Errands marketplaces retain through trusted, repeat relationships. Identical reviews/bios make reputation useless for selection, and the absence of rebook/favorites forces every repeat job back through random matching — suppressing the repeat-purchase loop that drives LTV.
suggestion: Store per-pal reviews with a rating breakdown/completion/response rate and badges; add 'Request again'/'Rebook [pal]' CTAs on OrderComplete and FavorHistoryDetail; and add a favorites list supporting direct-request to a specific pal.
concreteFix: Three changes, biggest value first: (1) Per-pal bio + reviews. In ProviderDetail replace the hardcoded bio block (providers.tsx:162-165) with `{pal.bio}` (data already exists). Convert mockData `reviews` to per-pal: either add `palId` to Review (types/index.ts:112-119) and filter at providers.tsx:169 by `pal.id`, or make it `reviewsByPal: Record<string, Review[]>`; seed 2-3 distinct reviews per pal id. (2) Rebook CTA. Add a "REQUEST AGAIN" button on FavorHistoryDetail (history.tsx, above/with the support box at 305-321) and on OrderComplete (tracking.tsx, after submit) that calls `s.setDraft({ tier, description, price, location, palId })` from the historical favor and navigates into the request flow (e.g. FavorSummary) pre-filled, optionally routing match directly to that palId. (3) Favorites. Add `favorites: string[]` + `toggleFavorite(palId)` to the store (store/index.tsx) and a heart toggle on ProviderDetail header (providers.tsx:126-136); optionally surface a Favorites list that supports direct-request to a specific pal. Item (1)+(2) are the M-sized, high-impact core; (3) is the L extension.
evidence: src/screens/providers.tsx:9,162-187 (global seedReviews + hardcoded bio ignoring pal.bio); src/data/mockData.ts:60-64 (single flat reviews array, no palId) and :40,45,50,55 (per-pal bios that exist but are unused); src/screens/history.tsx:305-321 (support box, no rebook); src/screens/tracking.tsx:171-254 (OrderComplete ends at feedback, no rebook); src/types/index.ts:112-119 (Review has no palId) + no favorites type; src/store/index.tsx (no favorites state/methods)

title: Forced single-role choice and a hard signup wall before any value is shown
observation: The first interaction forces a permanent-feeling binary — ASK A FAVOR vs BE A FAVOR PAL — then routes straight to SignupLogin (onboarding.tsx:60-101), and every screen is gated behind isAuthenticated (navigation/index.tsx:54-57). Yet the app treats every user as both roles via a role toggle on the dashboard/profile/tracking, so the upfront either/or is misleading.
impact: Forcing account creation before demonstrating any value (no visible prices, ETAs, coverage, or nearby-pal count pre-auth) depresses top-of-funnel conversion, and the false either/or can deter the large segment of pals-who-also-need-favors from completing signup.
suggestion: Let users preview the map, tiers, and nearby-pal count before auth; reframe Welcome as 'what would you like to do first?' (a starting action, not a fixed identity); and defer pal-specific vetting until they choose to go online.
concreteFix: Scope the fix to the verifiable bug: make the Welcome role choice actually carry through. In store/index.tsx add an intendedRole state (or reuse pendingSignup). Have Welcome.pick (onboarding.tsx:64) call a new setIntendedRole(role) instead of relying on the no-op setRole. Then in both login (store:96) and verifyOtp (store:87), create the user with role: intendedRole ?? seed.currentUser.role so a user who tapped 'BE A FAVOR PAL' lands on the pal dashboard (and, if pal, you can also default status to 'online'). This requires no Figma deviation. Treat the larger 'show map/tiers/nearby-pal count before auth and reframe Welcome as a starting action' suggestion as a separate product proposal to raise with the owner, since it changes the Figma-faithful onboarding screens and its conversion claims are unverifiable in a mock build.
evidence: src/screens/onboarding.tsx:64-67 (pick→setRole); src/store/index.tsx:107 (setRole no-op when user null), :87 & :96 (verifyOtp/login build user from currentUser), src/data/mockData.ts:28 (role:'member' hardcoded); src/navigation/index.tsx:57 (isAuthenticated gate)

title: No growth loops: no referral program, no first-favor incentive, and the SHARE button is dead
observation: The only viral surface, the SHARE tab, has no onPress (tracking.tsx:137-140). There is no referral program, promo/credit, or first-favor discount anywhere in the store or types, and the 'Other' tip option collects nothing.
impact: Local marketplaces grow most cheaply through geo-clustered referrals and need an incentive to force the critical first transaction. With none, CAC stays fully paid, first-favor activation is unaided, and the one built-in share affordance is a dead end.
suggestion: Add a two-sided referral ('give $10, get $10') with codes/credits, a first-favor promo on the dashboard and the no-supply empty state, and wire SHARE to a referral deep link.
concreteFix: In-scope quick wins (S): (a) In tracking.tsx, import Share from 'react-native' and add onPress to the SHARE TouchableOpacity (:137) calling Share.share({ message: 'I just booked a favor on My Favor — <deep link>' }); likewise wire the dead ACTIVITY tab (:151) to navigate to History. (b) In OrderComplete, when tipKey==='other' render a small numeric Field below the chip row, parse it into tip, and pass that custom value into s.rateFavor (currently 'Other' submits an undefined tip silently). Out-of-scope/L (do not bundle as a defect): a referral program would require new store state and types fields (referralCode, credits, promo) plus dashboard/empty-state surfaces — flag as a product backlog item since it does not exist in the Figma design, rather than a fix.
evidence: src/screens/tracking.tsx:137-140 (dead SHARE), :151-154 (dead ACTIVITY), :164-169 + :218 ('Other' tip = undefined, no input); grep of src for referral/promo/credit/discount = 0 matches (no referral infra in store/types)

title: Every heading is set in Comfortaa, but the Figma uses a crisp bold geometric sans throughout
observation: typography.ts:20-25 maps display/h1-h6 to Comfortaa_700Bold/500Medium and claims it 'matches' the design, but every reference artboard (launch, select-favor, favor-summary, order-complete, earnings, favor-booked) uses a sharp double-story-'a' geometric sans (Poppins/SF-Display). Comfortaa's single-story 'a', circular bowls and wide tracking read materially softer/bubblier on every screen, including the 44px wordmark (onboarding.tsx:49).
impact: Typeface is the loudest brand carrier; swapping the entire display scale to a rounded face shifts the app's personality away from the approved design on every screen at once — the single largest systemic visual deviation.
suggestion: Register the design's sans (Poppins or SF/Inter) and point fonts.display at its Bold/SemiBold weights; keep Comfortaa only where a frame truly uses it. Re-check line-heights after the swap since Comfortaa runs wider.
concreteFix: 1) Register Poppins: add @expo-google-fonts/poppins (Poppins_500Medium, Poppins_600SemiBold, Poppins_700Bold) to the useFonts() call in App.tsx:17-20,27-36. 2) In src/theme/typography.ts:6-8, repoint fonts.display -> 'Poppins_700Bold' and fonts.displayMedium -> 'Poppins_500Medium' (or SemiBold for h4); remove the misleading 'matches the design' comment at lines 1-3. Keep the Comfortaa import only if an audit finds a frame that genuinely uses it (none of the reviewed artboards do — likely drop it). 3) Re-tune the display/h1-h6 lineHeights at typography.ts:20-25 after the swap: Poppins sits narrower and a touch shorter than Comfortaa, so verify the 44px wordmark (onboarding.tsx:49-51, lineHeight 54) and the long topbar titles ('Earning History') don't clip or look loose. 4) Optionally extend the same Poppins family to body/label variants to match the design's apparent single-family system, but at minimum the display scale must change.
evidence: src/theme/typography.ts:6-8,20-25 (Comfortaa mapping + false 'matches' comment); App.tsx:17-20,34-35 (Comfortaa registration/load); onboarding.tsx:49-51 (44px wordmark via variant='display'); evidence: figma-ref/launch.png, select-favor.png, order-complete.png, earnings.png

area: Pal flow / Profile / SideDrawer / Payouts / Dashboard
title: Large parts of the app bypass the Txt/type system and render in the OS system font
observation: Member request/checkout/auth screens use Txt (Open Sans via tokens), but the entire pal flow and profile/drawer screens use raw <Text> with hardcoded fontWeight/fontSize and no fontFamily (pal.tsx:81,122,127,173,253,263; profile.tsx Stat 165, InfoRow 179-180, DrawerRow 477; payouts.tsx 282,85), so RN falls back to San Francisco/Roboto. Body copy silently changes typeface crossing from member to pal/profile, and each screen re-derives its own size/weight ramp.
impact: Two different body fonts coexist in one product and the type ramp drifts screen-to-screen, undercutting the otherwise high-fidelity build — and it means the Comfortaa swap above would only fix half the app.
suggestion: Replace raw <Text> with <Txt variant=...> (or at minimum add fontFamily from tokens) across pal.tsx, profile.tsx, payouts.tsx, dashboard.tsx, and add dark-friendly Txt color props instead of hand-set hex.
concreteFix: Limit the fix to the real offenders: pal.tsx and profile.tsx. Lowest-effort path that preserves the bespoke dark palette: import { fonts } from '../theme' and add the matching family to each inline/StyleSheet text style (fontWeight '700'->fonts.bodyBold, '600'->fonts.bodySemiBold, '400'->fonts.bodyRegular; the 800-weight stat/number styles can keep Comfortaa via fonts.display for a display feel). Cleaner path: replace the raw <Text> with <Txt variant=... color="#fff"|SUBTLE> mapping each size/weight to the nearest existing variant (e.g. the 18px bold names -> h6, the 14px subtle metadata -> caption/bodySm, section titles -> label). Either way, keep the explicit dark color props since these screens are not theme-driven. Explicitly EXCLUDE payouts.tsx (already correct; leave the Apple Pay 'Pay' badge as raw Text since it imitates a logo) and dashboard.tsx (already 100% Txt) from the change, contrary to the finding's suggestion. Optionally add a `weight` prop to Txt so dark screens can pick body weights without hand-set fontFamily.
evidence: Confirmed: src/theme/typography.ts:19-32 (fontFamily per variant), App.tsx:27-35 (fonts loaded), src/components/index.tsx:52-54 (Txt applies tokens). Offenders: src/screens/pal.tsx:81,122,127,148,173,202-203,213-215,253,263; src/screens/profile.tsx:165,179-180,477. Overstated/already-correct: src/screens/payouts.tsx (only raw Text at 72/style 282; rest use Txt at 60,82,124,130,137,168,178,189,225,248) and src/screens/dashboard.tsx (0 raw Text).

title: Dark-surface palette is fragmented: the same roles use 4-6 near-duplicate hex values
observation: colors.ts:36-40 defines darkBg/darkSurface/darkSurfaceAlt/darkBorder but almost no dark screen uses them. Canvas: #0C0C0C vs #000000 vs #0D0A0A; navy sheets: #141A24 / #1C2331 / #262C40 / #171922; secondary text: #9BA1A6 / #9AA3B2 / #8C8C8C / #8A8A8A / #ADB5BD; dividers span six values (#262626/#262C38/#2C3647/#2B2B2B/#2E3647/#1C1C1C).
impact: The pal, member-tracking, profile and payouts flows are all 'dark sheet over dark map' yet each uses subtly different blacks, navies and grays, so the dark surfaces never feel like one system — low-level inconsistency that erodes perceived quality.
suggestion: Promote the dark values to named tokens (darkCanvas, darkSheet, darkSheetAlt, darkSubtext, darkDivider) and consume them everywhere; collapse the 5 secondary-gray and 6 divider variants to one each.
concreteFix: In src/theme/colors.ts, add named dark tokens to `palette` and rationalize the set: darkCanvas (pick one map/black, e.g. #0C0C0C for sheets and #131820 for the map backdrop — decide one each), darkSheet (#1C2331), darkSheetAlt (#141A24), darkSubtext (collapse the 7 grays to one, e.g. #9AA3B2), darkDivider (collapse the 6 to one, e.g. #2C3647). Then replace the module-level constants that currently re-declare these: pal.tsx:11-17 (DARK_BG/SHEET/SHEET_ALT/SUBTLE/DIVIDER), profile.tsx:16-23 (DARK_BG/FIELD_BG/NAVY/SUBTLE/DARK_DIVIDER plus the inline #8C8C8C at :180 and #2E3647 at :572), payouts.tsx:17-20 (BLACK/FIELD_NAVY/DARK_DIVIDER/SUBTLE_GRAY plus inline #1C1C1C at :270 and #9AA1B2 at :248), messages.tsx:125-130 (bg/bar/placeholder), dashboard.tsx:13-14 (MAP_BG/BAR_BG). tracking.tsx already consumes palette.dark* — extend it to use the new darkSubtext for SUBTEXT (#9AA3B2). Keep the two intentional layering tones (sheet vs sheetAlt) but make every screen draw from the same two tokens. Verify visually by launching the app on the pal/tracking/profile/payouts/messages dark screens.
evidence: src/theme/colors.ts:36-40,103-104; src/screens/pal.tsx:11-17; src/screens/profile.tsx:16-23,180,572; src/screens/payouts.tsx:17-20,248,270; src/screens/messages.tsx:125-130; src/screens/dashboard.tsx:13-14; src/screens/tracking.tsx:12-15

title: Disabled button state is off-spec and inconsistent across screens
observation: The shared Button's disabled treatment is opacity:0.5 over the near-black cta (components:102), rendering medium-dark gray, but Figma's disabled CTAs are a light neutral-gray pill (select-favor NEXT, order-complete SUBMIT FEEDBACK). SelectFavor uses the default (request.tsx:138), OrderComplete overrides to '#C4C4C4' (tracking.tsx:247-250), Help uses the default again — three different grays, none matching spec.
impact: Disabled primary actions are a high-frequency state at the bottom of nearly every form; rendering them inconsistently (and darker than designed) makes gating feel arbitrary and slightly broken.
suggestion: Bake a single disabled style into Button (light-gray fill + muted label, matching Figma) and delete the per-screen OrderComplete override.
concreteFix: In components/index.tsx Button: stop using opacity for the disabled state. Add a disabled token (e.g. disabledBtn: '#C4C4C4' in src/theme/colors.ts) and, when disabled and variant !== 'ghost', set backgroundColor to that token with opacity:1 and keep white/muted label text (matching OrderComplete's current #C4C4C4 + white). Then delete the per-screen override at tracking.tsx:247-250 so OrderComplete becomes style={{ marginTop: tokens.spacing.lg }}. This makes SelectFavor, Help, pal rating, and all other gated CTAs render the single spec-correct light-gray pill.
evidence: src/components/index.tsx:102 (disabled opacity over theme.cta); src/theme/colors.ts:13 (cta '#141414'); src/screens/tracking.tsx:247-250 (one-off #C4C4C4 override); src/screens/request.tsx:138 and src/screens/profile.tsx:397-403 (default off-spec disabled); figma-ref/select-favor.png

title: Map rendering swings between a rich stylized 'streets' look and a clip-art placeholder
observation: Dashboard and pal/tracking render an elaborate stylized dark map (dashboard.tsx DarkMap:19-76; pal.tsx MapBackdrop:22-32), but the member request flow falls back to the bare MapPlaceholder — a flat baby-blue panel with a literal red 'map' Ionicon centered behind the content (components:230-239) — on ConfirmAddress (request.tsx:331), Searching (checkout.tsx:266) and ProviderResults (providers.tsx:42), whereas the designs show real street maps.
impact: The request flow is the core conversion path; a clip-art placeholder there, next to a richly stylized dashboard map, reads as half-built and breaks the spatial illusion the design relies on.
suggestion: Until a real map SDK lands, route every map screen through one shared stylized backdrop (reuse DarkMap or a single light-street variant); at minimum drop the centered 'map' glyph from MapPlaceholder when children are provided.
concreteFix: Two-part, design-aligned fix. (1) Kill the stray glyph: in components/index.tsx MapPlaceholder, only render the centered Ionicons "map" + label when no children are passed, e.g. wrap lines 234-235 in {!children && (...)}. This alone removes the floating red map icon behind the funnel pins. (2) Match the Figma's light street map instead of reusing DarkMap: add a lightweight LightMap backdrop (mirror the DarkMap approach in dashboard.tsx:19-76 but with light tones — base ~#EDEFF2, grey road lines rgba(0,0,0,0.06), one muted amber arterial, a pale water wedge) and render it as the MapPlaceholder background (or expose a variant prop) for ConfirmAddress, Searching and ProviderResults. Do NOT switch those screens to the dark DarkMap — confirm-address.png/searching.png/provider-results.png are light maps in the design; only the dashboard/pal/tracking screens are dark.
evidence: src/components/index.tsx:230-239 (MapPlaceholder always renders centered red glyph); used bare at src/screens/request.tsx:331, src/screens/checkout.tsx:266, src/screens/providers.tsx:42 — vs stylized src/screens/dashboard.tsx:19-76 and src/screens/pal.tsx:22-32. Design refs: figma-ref/confirm-address.png, searching.png, provider-results.png (light maps) vs figma-ref/dashboard.png (dark map).

title: Back icon alternates between chevron-back and arrow-back; Figma uses arrow-back
observation: TopBar (components:215), profile DarkHeader (profile.tsx:32) and payouts DarkTopBar (payouts.tsx:55) use Ionicons 'chevron-back', while CheckoutHeader (checkout.tsx:56) and MessageThread (messages.tsx:163) use 'arrow-back'. The references consistently show a full arrow, and the glyph even changes shape mid-flow (chevron on SelectFavor -> arrow on FavorSummary).
impact: The back button is the most-used control; changing shape between adjacent screens is a small but pervasive consistency tell that screens were built by different hands.
suggestion: Standardize all header components on 'arrow-back' (matching Figma), ideally via a single shared header so the affordance is defined once.
concreteFix: Swap the glyph in the three deviating headers from 'chevron-back' to 'arrow-back': components/index.tsx:215 (TopBar), payouts.tsx:55 (DarkTopBar), profile.tsx:32 (DarkHeader). Leave checkout.tsx:56 and messages.tsx:163 as-is (already arrow-back, matching Figma). Size 26 already matches across all of them, so only the name prop changes. Optional follow-up: the dark headers (DarkTopBar, DarkHeader) and the light TopBar are near-duplicate layouts differing mainly in color; consolidating them into one TopBar that takes a variant/color prop would define the back affordance once and prevent this drift recurring, but the minimal correct fix is the three name swaps.
evidence: src/components/index.tsx:215; src/screens/payouts.tsx:55; src/screens/profile.tsx:32 (chevron-back to fix) vs src/screens/checkout.tsx:56 and src/screens/messages.tsx:163 (already arrow-back); SelectFavor wiring at src/screens/request.tsx:108; Figma proof figma-ref/select-favor.png, favor-summary.png, earnings.png

title: Mirror-image 'Thank You' screens diverge in title scale, font and hero art
observation: Same pattern (thank-you + star rating + feedback) styled inconsistently: member 'Thank You!' is variant display 40px Comfortaa with celebration.png and a Txt 'Rating' label (tracking.tsx:189); pal 'Thank You!' is variant h2 26px over launch-people.png with 'Rating' as a raw 22px system-font Text (pal.tsx:249,253). The headline jumps 40px->26px and the body switches Open Sans->system font.
impact: Both sides hit a completion screen at the emotional peak of the flow; they should feel like one designed moment, not two loosely related forms with different title sizes and fonts.
suggestion: Extract a shared CompletionScreen (title size, heading font, rating row, feedback box) and parameterize only the copy, hero asset, and whether tip chips appear.
concreteFix: Do NOT extract a shared CompletionScreen and do NOT bump the pal title to 40px — keep h2 (26px) and the dark treatment to stay faithful to figma-ref/pal-complete.png. Instead, make PalFavorComplete use the design typography: replace the raw <Text style={{ fontSize: 22, fontWeight: '700' }}>Rating</Text> at pal.tsx:253 with <Txt variant=\"h4\" color=\"#fff\"> to match the member 'Rating' label, and likewise route the 'Tell us about your experience' label (pal.tsx:263) and the feedback TextInput (pal.tsx:266) through tokens.typography (e.g. apply fonts.bodyRegular) so the pal completion screen renders in Comfortaa/Open Sans rather than the platform system font. Optionally swap the pal StarRating (pal.tsx:255-259) for the shared StarRating component to match the member side.
evidence: src/screens/pal.tsx:249-263 (PalFavorComplete title + raw-Text 'Rating'/label); src/screens/tracking.tsx:189-205 (OrderComplete title + Txt 'Rating'); src/theme/typography.ts:20-24

title: Black shadows on dark surfaces give no elevation; CTA radius/height drift where buttons are re-implemented
observation: tokens.shadow.card uses shadowColor '#000' (spacing.ts:23-31) applied on near-black surfaces (dashboard RadiusPin:103; dark-theme Card components:164), where it's invisible so elements read flat. Separately the shared Button is height 54 / radius 12 (components:292) but hand-rolled CTAs differ: pal radius 14 (pal.tsx:300-302), checkout payBtn height 56 / radius 14 (checkout.tsx:354-355).
impact: Dark sheets that should float above the map look pasted-on, and the same 'primary button' isn't pixel-identical across the app — small depth/consistency cues a designer would catch.
suggestion: Add an elevated-on-dark shadow token (lighter shadow + subtle top border) for dark sheets, and replace hand-rolled white/black CTAs with the shared Button so radius 12 / height 54 is enforced everywhere.
concreteFix:
1) Elevation: the productive fix is NOT a blanket 'dark shadow token.' Instead (a) add a shadow to the only genuinely-flat dark sheets — pal.tsx st.sheet and st.scrollSheet (pal.tsx:292-293) — mirroring providers.tsx:212-216 (shadowColor #000, offset {0,-8}, opacity 0.18, radius 16, elevation 12) so they cast onto the light map; (b) for dark mode only, give Card a subtle top hairline (e.g. borderColor a lighter darkBorder) in components/index.tsx:164 since #000 shadows can't show on #131820 — or accept it given dark mode is a minor toggle.
2) CTA consistency: replace pal.tsx whiteBtn/blackBtn (pal.tsx:300-302 and their usages) with the shared <Button variant='white'/> and <Button variant='primary'/>, which enforce radius 12 / height 54 and were built for dark sheets (components:68). Leave checkout.tsx payBtn AS-IS — it is an intentional distinct blue secure-pay button, not the primary CTA.
evidence: src/screens/dashboard.tsx:100-104 (pin shadow on light map); src/components/index.tsx:164,292; src/theme/colors.ts:98-101 (dark theme surfaces); src/screens/profile.tsx:318 (dark toggle); src/screens/pal.tsx:292-293 (sheets, no shadow),300-302 (radius 14 CTAs); src/screens/checkout.tsx:238-243,354-356 (blue PAY_BLUE pay button, not the primary CTA)

area: Role switch / Signup terms / tier-tip-payment selectors / SetStatus
title: Custom toggles, checkboxes and selection controls never expose their state or role to assistive tech
observation: Selection state is conveyed only visually. The role switch is a plain TouchableOpacity with no role/state (dashboard.tsx:163-170); the Signup terms box has no role=checkbox/checked (authflow.tsx:263-280); same gap on SelectFavor tiers (request.tsx:46-82), tip chips (tracking.tsx:215-224), payment tiles (checkout.tsx:212-235), BankInfo account-type (payouts.tsx:230-251), SetStatus radios (profile.tsx:504-515). Notably Profile/Settings use the native accessible <Switch>, so the pattern is inconsistent.
impact: A screen-reader user can't tell whether they're a Member or Pal, whether they accepted the Terms (a legal gate), which tier/card/tip is selected, or which status is active — and selected-by-color-only fails WCAG 1.4.1.
suggestion: Use accessibilityRole='switch'/'checkbox'/'radio' with accessibilityState={{checked|selected}} on these controls (and 'radiogroup' on containers); prefer reusing the native Switch for the dashboard toggle.
concreteFix: Add accessibilityRole plus accessibilityState to each control. Dashboard role switch: accessibilityRole "switch", accessibilityState checked:isPal, accessibilityLabel pillText (or reuse the native Switch component). Authflow terms: accessibilityRole "checkbox", accessibilityState checked:agree. Tracking tip chips, checkout payment tiles, payouts account-type, profile SetStatus: accessibilityRole "radio", accessibilityState selected:(active/isSel/sel) with the enclosing container marked accessibilityRole "radiogroup". For SelectFavor tiers, extend the shared Card component (components/index.tsx:161) to accept and forward accessibilityRole/accessibilityState/accessibilityLabel onto its TouchableOpacity, then pass radio semantics from request.tsx. On RN Web these map to the correct ARIA roles automatically. Effort M because it touches 6 screens plus a shared-component prop change and warrants a manual screen-reader pass.
evidence: src/screens/dashboard.tsx:163-170; src/screens/authflow.tsx:263-280; src/screens/request.tsx:46-82 plus src/components/index.tsx:161-166; src/screens/tracking.tsx:215-224; src/screens/checkout.tsx:216-231; src/screens/payouts.tsx:230-249; src/screens/profile.tsx:507-513

title: Gray placeholder/tertiary text fails WCAG contrast, and several fields use the placeholder as the only label
observation: placeholderTextColor is textTertiary #959595 on #EFEFEF fields = 2.60:1, below AA. It's the placeholder for every Field (components:146), auth password fields, and the description/feedback textareas — worse where it's the only label: FavorDescription/Negotiate render a multiline Field with no label (request.tsx:182-188,289-295), and the Account screen renders body text in #8A909B on white at 3.21:1 (payouts.tsx:168-178).
impact: Low-vision users and anyone in sunlight cannot read the only cue telling them what a field is for; with no persistent label, fields become unidentifiable. Fails WCAG 1.4.3 and 3.3.2.
suggestion: Darken placeholder to ~#6B7280 (≈4.8:1 on #EFEFEF), never use a placeholder as the sole label (add a persistent Txt label above the description/feedback fields), and bump the #8A909B Account text to clear 4.5:1.
concreteFix: 1) colors.ts: add a dedicated token e.g. placeholder/fieldHint ~#67707E (verify >=4.5:1 on #EFEFEF; #6B7280 is only 4.20:1 so go slightly darker) and use it for placeholderTextColor in components/index.tsx:146 — do NOT just darken textTertiary, which also colors field/camera icons (request.tsx:198) and would drift from Figma. 2) payouts.tsx StripeOnboarding: replace the #8A909B body-text literals on lines 168 and 178 with ~#5A6271 (6.14:1 on white) or #525A66 (6.97:1, already used on line 188 for 'Earning History', for consistency); the chevron/icon-only #8A909B uses (lines 167,179) are non-text and can stay. 3) Optional, for SR support: pass the visible 'Describe the favor you need.' string as the Field's label prop or add accessibilityLabel on the description/Negotiate Fields (request.tsx:182-188, 289-295) so the existing visible label is programmatically associated — this is a screen-reader nicety, not a fix for 'unidentifiable' fields since a visible label already exists.
evidence: src/components/index.tsx:146; src/theme/colors.ts:29,33; src/screens/payouts.tsx:166-180; src/screens/request.tsx:178-188,286-295

area: Star ratings / password eye / payment edit-delete / text links
title: Multiple interactive targets fall well below the 44x44 minimum, some packed tightly together
observation: Selectable stars wrap only the bare icon with no padding/hitSlop: 20px (components:192-194), 28px (tracking.tsx:204), 30px (pal.tsx:255-259), with a 2px gap so adjacent stars nearly touch. The password eye is ~40px (authflow.tsx:49). The Payment edit (pencil) and delete (trash) are 22px icons with hitSlop 8 sitting directly adjacent (payment.tsx:46-51) — a mis-tap deletes a card. 'Forgot Password?' is ~15px text with hitSlop 8.
impact: Rating a pal and toggling password visibility are core actions; sub-44px targets and adjacent tiny icons cause mis-taps for users with motor/tremor impairments, and an accidental card deletion has no undo. Fails WCAG 2.5.5/2.5.8.
suggestion: Give each selectable star padding + hitSlop to reach 44x44, raise eye-toggle/link hitSlop to ~12 each side, separate the edit/delete icons and add a confirm before removeCard, and add accessibilityRole='adjustable'/per-star labels in StarRating.
concreteFix: payment.tsx:49 is the highest value fix: gate removeCard behind a confirm, reusing the existing Alert.alert pattern at profile.tsx:296 with a Cancel button and a destructive Remove button that calls removeCard(card.id). Increase separation between the pencil and trash icons by raising marginRight to about 24 and using asymmetric hitSlop, and add accessibilityLabels for edit and delete. In components/index.tsx:192-194, in the onChange branch, give the TouchableOpacity a larger hitSlop or padding, accessibilityRole button, and a per-star accessibilityLabel; this also fixes the 28px stars in tracking.tsx:204. Apply the same hitSlop to the inline 30px stars at pal.tsx:255-259. Optional AAA polish: bump the eye toggle hitSlop at authflow.tsx:49 from 10 to 12 and the Forgot Password link at authflow.tsx:96-104 from 8 to 12.
evidence: src/screens/payment.tsx:46-51 (no-confirm removeCard + adjacent icons); src/components/index.tsx:185-199 (StarRating onChange has no hitSlop); src/screens/tracking.tsx:204 (28px selectable); src/screens/pal.tsx:255-259 (30px selectable); src/screens/authflow.tsx:49,96-104 (eye/link hitSlop); src/store/index.tsx:170 (removeCard has no undo)

area: History status badges / status dots / 'View More' links
title: State is communicated by color alone, on badges whose contrast is below AA
observation: Status pills have 12px text below AA: Completed #0A8F08 on #E4F8E4 = 3.81:1, Cancelled #ED1C24 on #FCE3E4 = 3.60:1 (history.tsx:58-79,127-131). Availability is a bare colored dot with no text (profile.tsx:538; SetStatus 'Invisible' is only a transparent ring), tappable 'View More' is distinguished only by red (tracking.tsx:82, history.tsx:110), and the Welcome '$$' is #02CB00 on white = 2.20:1 (onboarding.tsx:80).
impact: Colorblind and low-vision users can't reliably read the status pill or distinguish online/invisible/offline, and red-only links aren't perceivable as links. Fails WCAG 1.4.1 and 1.4.3.
suggestion: Darken badge foregrounds/tints to clear 4.5:1 at 12px, pair status dots/badges with a text label or icon shape, give links an underline or role='link', and darken the emphasized green '$$'.
concreteFix: Scope the fix to the verified contrast issues. (1) In history.tsx statusMeta (lines 58-79) darken the badge foregrounds to >=4.5:1 on their tints: Completed fg to ~#0A6B05, Cancelled fg to ~#C20E15 (already the brandDark token) instead of theme.danger, the amber group to ~#8A5E00, and Requested to ~#005FCC; or alternatively darken nothing and instead deepen the tints. (2) In onboarding.tsx:80 stop using the raw theme.success (#02CB00) for the emphasized '$$' — introduce a darker emphasis green (e.g. ~#009900, which clears 3:1 for the 28px bold text) or reuse it only where >=3:1. (3) For the tracking.tsx:82 "View More" red link add `textDecorationLine: 'underline'` (and add an accessibilityRole on its TouchableOpacity); optionally make history.tsx:110 "View More" use the same underlined-link treatment so it reads as tappable. The status-dot / SetStatus portions need no change.
evidence: history.tsx:58-79 (statusMeta colors) and :128 (12px badge text); onboarding.tsx:80 (theme.success '$$'); colors.ts:19 (success #02CB00); tracking.tsx:82 (red View More, no underline); profile.tsx:538 (hardcoded RED statusDot), :485-489 + :510 (SetStatus options have text labels)

title: Unbounded lists use ScrollView + .map() with no virtualization; the chat thread is the worst offender
observation: Every list renders its entire dataset eagerly with ScrollView + Array.map (messages.tsx:63-70,172-190; history.tsx:91-136; payouts.tsx:118-145) — no FlatList, no windowing/recycling — and rows allocate a fresh onPress closure + inline style arrays per item. The chat thread re-maps every bubble and calls scrollToEnd on every onContentSizeChange (messages.tsx:176). Tiny seed data masks it today.
impact: Favor history, earnings, and conversations are inherently unbounded; with ScrollView a member with 300 past favors mounts 300 row subtrees (each with an Avatar Image) on entry, spiking memory/TTI and causing scroll jank; the chat scrollToEnd gets quadratic as history grows.
suggestion: Convert to FlatList/FlashList with keyExtractor, memoized renderItem, and getItemLayout; make MessageThread an inverted FlatList (drop manual scrollToEnd); use SectionList for the Earnings month grouping.
concreteFix: Convert the four list call-sites to virtualized lists, deferring until a real data source is wired (low priority for the prototype). (1) messages.tsx Messages - replace ScrollView+threads.map with FlatList: data=threads, keyExtractor=t.id, renderItem rendering a React.memo-wrapped ThreadRow, ListEmptyComponent for the empty state. (2) messages.tsx MessageThread - use an inverted FlatList over a reversed messages array, drop the onContentSizeChange/scrollToEnd hack entirely (inverted lists auto-pin to newest), memoize Bubble, and recompute the tail grouping against reversed order. (3) history.tsx History - FlatList with data=s.history, keyExtractor=h.id, memoized renderItem, ListEmptyComponent. (4) payouts.tsx Earnings - SectionList fed by groupByMonth mapped to {title,data}, with renderSectionHeader for the month title + divider and renderItem for the earn row. Skip getItemLayout where row heights are variable.
evidence: messages.tsx:63-70 (Messages list), messages.tsx:172-190 and :176 (MessageThread scrollToEnd), messages.tsx:86 (un-memoized ThreadRow), messages.tsx:215 (un-memoized Bubble); history.tsx:91-136 (History list); payouts.tsx:118-145 (Earnings groups.map/items.map)

title: Dimensions captured once at module load is not responsive to web resize / rotation / split-view
observation: SCREEN_H/W are read once at module-evaluation time and frozen (providers.tsx:11; request.tsx:15; checkout.tsx:20; dashboard.tsx:10), driving full-bleed map heights and absolute pin offsets (MapPlaceholder height={SCREEN_H}, providers.tsx:42; checkout.tsx:267-271).
impact: The app targets web + Expo Go; resizing the browser window never updates these values, so the map keeps its first-paint height and under/overflows after resize — same on tablet rotation/Android split-screen. The first web measurement can also be wrong before layout settles.
suggestion: Use the useWindowDimensions() hook inside each component, size maps with flex:1 / absoluteFill instead of pixel heights, and express pin positions as percentages.
concreteFix: Replace the module-level Dimensions.get('window') constants in dashboard.tsx, providers.tsx, checkout.tsx, request.tsx with the useWindowDimensions() hook called inside each component so values re-render on resize. Better, give MapPlaceholder a flex escape hatch or default it to StyleSheet.absoluteFill when no height is passed, render the four full-bleed maps inside a flex:1 container, and drop the height prop. Convert the remaining pixel pin offsets at providers.tsx lines 43-45 and checkout.tsx lines 267-271 to percentage positions like request.tsx already uses. This locks maps to the viewport across web resize and Android split-screen.
evidence: src/screens/providers.tsx:11,42-45,95; src/screens/dashboard.tsx:10,140; src/screens/checkout.tsx:20,266-271; src/screens/request.tsx:15,331; src/components/index.tsx:230-238

title: The slider drives a full-screen re-render on every drag tick and depends on a community slider for web
observation: onValueChange={setHours} updates state continuously while dragging (request.tsx:248-258), re-rendering the entire Negotiate screen each tick — including the multiline description TextInput and its live counter (request.tsx:289-298). The price-bubble position is recomputed from trackW each render, and @react-native-community/slider renders a limited-fidelity <input type=range> on web. (Lower priority: this screen is currently orphaned.)
impact: Dragging re-renders an unrelated TextInput on every step, dropping frames on low-end Android and risking IME/caret quirks; the web slider thumb/track may not match Figma.
suggestion: Isolate the slider + price bubble into a memoized subcomponent (drive the bubble with Animated/ref) so dragging doesn't re-render the description field; lift only the final value on slide complete; add a web style fallback.
concreteFix: If touched: extract the description Field + character counter (request.tsx:289-298) into a React.memo'd subcomponent (e.g. DescriptionField with value/onChangeText props) so slider drags no longer re-render the text field at all. That single change removes the only re-render the finding actually cares about, with no Animated/ref machinery needed. Optionally, if step granularity is ever increased, switch the commit to onSlidingComplete (keeping onValueChange only to move the bubble). For web pixel-fidelity of the thumb, add a Platform.select web style on the Slider's style prop; current tint props already render an acceptable black track/thumb, so this is optional polish, not required.
evidence: src/screens/request.tsx:218-306 (Negotiate); slider+onValueChange at 248-258, bubbleLeft compute at 228, description Field/counter at 289-298; reachability at request.tsx:96-100/129-134 and src/navigation/index.tsx:74

title: Oversized and inconsistently exported PNG assets relative to their render size
observation: launch-hero.png 353KB, celebration.png 312KB, launch-people.png 312KB, welcome-mower.png 234KB; notably tier-big.png is 156KB while its sibling tier illustrations are ~7-10KB despite all four rendering at a 60x60 thumbnail (request.tsx:60-63). celebration.png renders at height 300 (tracking.tsx:194-197) and launch-people.png at 320 (pal.tsx:250).
impact: On Expo Go and web these decode at full resolution regardless of display box, inflating memory and first-render time; tier-big.png at ~20x its siblings for an identical 60px slot is almost certainly a stray full-res export, and the bundle is larger than needed.
suggestion: Re-export tier illustrations at a consistent thumbnail resolution (@2x/@3x), compress the large celebration/launch PNGs (or ship WebP), confirm tier-big.png isn't a stray export, and consider expo-image for downsampling/caching.
concreteFix: 1) Re-export assets/img/request/tier-big.png at ~160-180px square (match its siblings) — drops it from 160KB to ~10KB. 2) Deduplicate the identical celebration/launch-people illustration: delete assets/img/tracking/celebration.png and point tracking.tsx:195 at the existing assets/img/onboarding/launch-people.png (or a shared const), saving ~320KB. 3) Leave launch-hero.png and the full-width illustrations at near-native resolution (they are correctly sized), but optionally ship them as WebP and render via expo-image for caching/decode savings. Drop the original 'decode regardless of display box' justification for the full-width images — it does not apply.
evidence: assets/img/request/tier-big.png (672x665, 160KB) vs tier-huge/small/tiny (120-160px, 7-10KB); request.tsx:60 + checkout.tsx:14-17 (60x60 slot); celebration.png == launch-people.png byte-identical (tracking.tsx:195, pal.tsx:19/250)

title: The flag emoji country chip is unreliable on web/Windows/older Android
observation: The phone field renders the regional-indicator flag emoji directly (profile.tsx:84, also Signup). Regional-indicator sequences are not rendered as flags by Windows Chrome (shown as 'US' letters) or some Android builds (tofu boxes).
impact: Because the app explicitly runs on web and Expo Go, the country selector degrades to 'US' text or empty boxes on common platforms — looking unfinished in the very form users edit their profile in.
suggestion: Replace the emoji flag with a small SVG/PNG flag (react-native-svg is already a dependency) or a country-picker library, or gate the emoji behind Platform.OS!=='web' with a text/SVG fallback.
concreteFix: Create one small reusable USFlag component using react-native-svg (already a dependency) rendering a simple stars-and-stripes glyph, or add a small PNG under assets/img and render it with Image; replace the Text emoji at profile.tsx:83 and authflow.tsx:246 with it. Lowest-effort alternative: gate the emoji behind a web Platform check with an SVG or PNG fallback. Since both chips are hardcoded US-only, a static SVG or PNG flag is cleanest.
evidence: src/screens/profile.tsx:83 (PhoneField, used by EditProfile at :252); src/screens/authflow.tsx:246 (Signup); react-native-svg dep at package.json:26

title: Counter says 'characters max' while the number counts down as you type
observation: The description fields render `{250 - desc.length} characters max.` (request.tsx:189-191,296-298), so the value falls 250->249->248 and literally reads '243 characters max.' mid-typing — a remaining-count wearing a maximum label. Other long-text fields use a fixed '700 characters max.' that never moves (tracking.tsx:238-240; profile.tsx:393-395; history.tsx:317-319), so the pattern is also inconsistent.
impact: The label contradicts its own number, so users can't tell if they're near a limit, and the inconsistency (some tick, some are static) makes the rule feel arbitrary.
suggestion: Standardize on a remaining-count phrasing everywhere ('{n} characters left' or '{used}/250') and apply the same live counter to the 700-char Feedback/Help/History fields.
concreteFix: Standardize on a clear remaining-count phrasing and apply it consistently. (1) In request.tsx change both labels (lines 190 and 297) from `{250 - desc.length} characters max.` to `{250 - desc.length} characters left.` (or `{desc.length}/250`). (2) For the static 700-char fields, make them live and matching: tracking.tsx:239 -> `{700 - feedback.length} characters left.`, profile.tsx:394 -> `{700 - msg.length} characters left.`, history.tsx:318 -> `{700 - message.length} characters left.` (each of those screens already holds the value in state: feedback, msg, message). Optionally factor a tiny `<CharCount value={text} max={n} />` helper in src/components/index.tsx so the phrasing can't drift again.
evidence: src/screens/request.tsx:189-191 and 296-298 (dynamic, mislabeled); src/screens/tracking.tsx:238-240, src/screens/profile.tsx:393-395, src/screens/history.tsx:317-319 (static 700)

title: The support message box reuses the favor-description placeholder
observation: The Help contact form's textarea placeholder is 'Provide as much detail as possible about your favor! Let your provider know about what they will be doing, what they will need to bring...' (profile.tsx:389) — the same coaching string for writing a favor request — and the identical placeholder is pasted into the 'Need help or have a question with this favor?' box on FavorHistoryDetail (history.tsx:315).
impact: In a support context the guidance is nonsensical (telling support 'what they will need to bring'), signaling copy-paste and lowering confidence that messages are read.
suggestion: Write context-appropriate placeholders: Help -> 'Tell us what's going on and how we can help.'; FavorHistoryDetail -> 'Describe the issue with this favor and we'll look into it.'; fix the header to 'Need help or have a question about this favor?'
concreteFix: Replace the two support-context placeholders with help-appropriate copy. At profile.tsx line 389 (Help form) use something like: Tell us what is going on and how we can help. At history.tsx line 315 (FavorHistoryDetail) use something like: Describe the issue with this favor and we will look into it. Also fix the FavorHistoryDetail header at history.tsx line 306 from with this favor to about this favor. Leave request.tsx line 148, the genuine favor-description coaching string, unchanged.
evidence: src/screens/profile.tsx:389 Help form placeholder; src/screens/history.tsx:315 FavorHistoryDetail placeholder and history.tsx:306 header grammar; origin string at src/screens/request.tsx:148

title: Inconsistent button casing and 'Okay' vs 'OK' across nearly identical actions
observation: Primary CTAs mix ALL-CAPS and Title case for the same kind of action: 'SUBMIT' (authflow.tsx:144,169) vs 'Submit'/'Done' (profile.tsx:367,398); 'Confirm Address' (request.tsx:383) and 'Save' (payouts.tsx:255) against caps-locked 'PAY NOW', 'NEXT', 'BOOK FAVOR PAL', 'SUBMIT FEEDBACK'. The Card Added modal confirms with 'Okay' (payment.tsx:166) instead of 'OK'.
impact: Casing is a voice signal; flipping between SHOUTING and Sentence case for equivalent buttons makes the product feel stitched together and weakens brand tone.
suggestion: Pick one rule and centralize it via the Button's existing uppercase prop (components:79); standardize the confirmation word to 'OK'/'Got it'.
concreteFix: Do NOT pursue app-wide casing standardization — it would break Figma fidelity (confirm-address.png/help.png/forgot-password.png each intentionally differ). Make only the targeted fidelity fix: in payment.tsx:166 change buttonLabel="Okay" to buttonLabel="OKAY" so the Card Added modal matches card-added.png. (InfoModal at components/index.tsx:283 hardcodes uppercase={false}, so passing the already-uppercase string is the safe path and won't affect other modals.) Leave all other CTAs as-is.
evidence: payment.tsx:166 (buttonLabel="Okay"); components/index.tsx:283 (InfoModal hardcodes uppercase={false}); components/index.tsx:79,112 (Button default uppercase=true + toUpperCase); request.tsx:383, profile.tsx:367/398, authflow.tsx:144 — compare figma-ref/card-added.png ("OKAY"), confirm-address.png ("Confirm Address"), help.png ("Submit"), forgot-password.png ("SUBMIT")

title: Inconsistent tab chrome and a mislabeled detail header with shared fake transaction data
observation: The four tab roots have four different headers: Home (map, hamburger), Messages (large title, no back — messages.tsx:43-61), History (TopBar WITH a back chevron though it's a tab root — history.tsx:90), Profile (dark header with back+pencil). Drilling from 'Favor History' opens a screen titled 'Payment History Detail' (history.tsx:168), and that detail hardcodes '3 Miles away' (history.tsx:236) and Transaction ID '1234abcde56fg' (history.tsx:281) identically for every favor.
impact: Back arrows on root tabs (nothing to pop) and clashing header styles make navigation feel unpredictable, and identical fake transaction IDs/distances on every receipt undermine trust in history records.
suggestion: Standardize tab-root headers (no back chevron on roots; consistent titles), rename the detail header to 'Favor Detail' (or align the list to 'Payment History'), and derive distance/transaction ID per-favor.
concreteFix: Keep the header title "Payment History Detail" and the History tab's back chevron exactly as-is (both are confirmed Figma-exact). The single worthwhile change: in src/screens/history.tsx around line 281, replace the literal '1234abcde56fg' with a stable per-favor value derived from favor.id (e.g. a normalized slice/hash of favor.id rendered as a transaction-ID-looking string) so each receipt shows a unique, consistent ID. Optionally leave '3 Miles away' (line 235) untouched since no per-favor distance is modeled and it matches the design. Do NOT rename the header or strip the back chevron.
evidence: src/screens/history.tsx:90 (TopBar back), :168 (detail title), :235-236 ('3 Miles away'), :281 (hardcoded txn ID); src/navigation/index.tsx:47 (History tab root); figma-ref/favor-history.png and figma-ref/payment-history-detail.png (both confirm back arrow + title)
